name: Acma
classification_notes: A narrow, highly contrasted sans-serif with pronounced stroke variation and expressive alternates, positioned by the foundry as display-first and categorised alongside humanist and expressive sans traditions.
summary: A high-contrast narrow sans with deep stroke variation and wide alternate characters, designed by Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins for display and editorial use.
description: Acma draws on Japanese modernist aesthetics and the fashion world, with its distinctive double-storey lowercase 'a' setting the tone for the family's tension between precision and fluidity. Ten weights from Thin to Black run with matching italics and a variable axis, while OpenType features include a second set of wide uppercase letters and alternate lowercase forms that allow unexpected compositional moments. Originally conceived for editorial headlines, the foundry positions it as usable from 12pt upwards.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Acma as an irreverent typeface with a powerful elegance, precise and subtle yet flowy and temperamental. Inspired by Japanese modernist aesthetics and the fashion world, it centres on luscious curves, wide alternates and deep contrast. The lowercase 'a' gave tone to the entire family, which they call a celebration of idealistic beauty expressed through tension, balance and unorthodox shapes. It comes in 10 weights plus italics and a variable cut, with 800 glyphs per style.

name: Agrandir
classification_notes: A serifless type family described as an antipode to neutral modernist grotesques, with deliberately quirky, unaligned letterforms that resist the clean geometry of pure geometric or humanist sans categories.
summary: A 74-style serifless family spanning five widths and a text cut, built around deliberately imperfect, quirky letterforms as a counterpoint to modernist neutrality.
description: Designed by Alex Slobzheninov, Agrandir runs across six sub-families from Tight to Grand, each with 14 styles plus a variable cut, alongside a four-style Text family for body copy. Three OpenType preset alternates, Default, Grotesk and Geometric, shift the overall character without requiring per-glyph adjustments. The tight spacing and wide width range make it well suited to headlines, logotypes and posters, while the Text subfamily handles longer reads.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Agrandir as a contemporary serifless family that celebrates the beauty of imperfection, designed as a brave antipode to neutral modernist fonts. They note that it accepts its own shapes as they are, unaligned, quirky and funky, celebrating humanity over machines, and offer it as 74 fonts across five widths and seven weights with italics, or as a single three-axis variable font.

name: Agrandir Grand
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The widest cut in the Agrandir system, sharing the family's alternate sets and Geometric / Grotesk presets.
summary: The widest width in Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir system, built for big, expressive headline settings.
description: The Grand width of Agrandir, drawn by Alex Slobzheninov as the most extended cut in the system. Fourteen styles span seven weights from Thin to Black with italics and 443 glyphs per weight. The same alternates and stylistic presets carry through, so Grand can sit beside the Normal, Wide and Tight widths in a single family hierarchy.
foundry_blurb: Pangram place Agrandir Grand inside the wider Agrandir system, framed as the family that flips modernist neutrality on its head. They credit the Agrandir collection to designer Alex Slobzheninov and describe it as built for humans, not machines, with 14 weights and 443 glyphs each at this width.

name: Agrandir Narrow
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The narrow cut in the Agrandir system, sharing the family's alternates and Geometric / Grotesk presets.
summary: The narrow cut of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, useful where you want personality at a tighter horizontal measure.
description: The Narrow width of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, drawn as a tighter sibling of Normal and Tight. Fourteen styles run from Thin to Black with italics and 443 glyphs each. The alternate sets, single-storey a and Geometric/Grotesk presets carry through, so Narrow can flex inside a wider Agrandir system.
foundry_blurb: Pangram set Agrandir Narrow alongside the rest of the Agrandir collection, describing the family as a contemporary serifless type system that flips modernist neutrality on its head. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer and note 14 weights and 443 glyphs each at this width.

name: Agrandir Normal
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The default width in the Agrandir system, holding the family's alternates and stylistic presets.
summary: The default width of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, the entry point into the wider system.
description: The Normal width of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, the family's default proportions. Fourteen styles span seven weights with italics and 443 glyphs each. Stylistic presets, single-storey a, alternate y, u, G, K and the straight-tail R run across the family, letting Normal sit cleanly with Tight, Narrow, Wide and Grand.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Agrandir as a contemporary serifless type family that celebrates the beauty of being imperfect. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer of the system and note that this width carries 14 weights and 443 glyphs each, with italic support and the family's alternate sets.

name: Agrandir Text
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. A four-style text cut tuned for body copy, drawn as a companion to the wider Agrandir system.
summary: The four-style text cut of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, tuned for running body copy.
description: The Text cut of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, drawn for body sizes rather than display. Four styles cover Regular and Bold with matching italics, sharing the family's alternates and presets so a website or magazine can run Agrandir display headlines into Agrandir Text paragraphs without breaking voice.
foundry_blurb: Pangram pitch Agrandir Text as the body-copy companion inside the Agrandir collection, describing the wider family as built for humans, not machines. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer and frame this cut as an intentional departure tuned for running text.

name: Agrandir Tight
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The tightest-spaced cut in the Agrandir system, drawn for big, dense headlines.
summary: The tightly-spaced cut of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, tuned for dense display settings.
description: The Tight width of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, the most compressed cut in the system. Fourteen styles span seven weights from Thin to Black with italics and 443 glyphs each. The family's alternates and presets carry through, so Tight handles posters and big display lockups while still talking to Normal and Wide in body settings.
foundry_blurb: Pangram place Agrandir Tight inside the Agrandir collection, describing the family as a contemporary serifless type system that flips modernist neutrality on its head. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer and note 14 weights with italic support at this tightest width.

name: Agrandir Wide
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The wide cut in the Agrandir system, sitting between Normal and Grand.
summary: The wide cut of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, sitting between Normal and Grand in the system.
description: The Wide width of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, drawn as a roomier sibling of Normal. Fourteen styles run from Thin to Black with italics and 443 glyphs each, carrying the family's alternates and Geometric/Grotesk presets. Useful where you want extra horizontal presence without going all the way to Grand.
foundry_blurb: Pangram set Agrandir Wide alongside the rest of the Agrandir collection, describing the family as built for humans, not machines. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer and note 14 weights and 443 glyphs each, with italic support, at this wider width.

name: Air
classification_notes: Pangram Pangram classify it as Grotesk and Sans Serif; the design draws on brutalist and minimalist influences with aerospace-inspired geometry, sitting firmly in the grotesque sans tradition.
summary: A grotesque sans in 36 styles originally designed by Mat Desjardins and expanded by Caio Kondo, spanning proportional and monospaced cuts with variable axes for weight, italic and mono.
description: Originally designed by Mat Desjardins and expanded by Caio Kondo of Inari Type, Air spans two subfamilies — proportional and monospaced — each offering nine upright weights from Thin to Black with matching italics and a variable font that unites all axes in a single file. The design takes visual cues from brutalism, minimalism and aerospace detailing, with a large character set of 656 glyphs including alternate characters, punctuation and arrows. Both cuts share the same weight range, making the family well-suited to mixed-setting work where editorial and code or tabular contexts need to coexist.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Air as a captivating fusion of brutalism and minimalism with a nod to aerospace design, originally created by Mat Desjardins and expanded by Caio Kondo. They highlight its massive character map, alternate characters, abundant punctuation and set of arrows, as well as 36 styles in total including a variable version that unites weight, italic and mono axes in a single file.

name: Air Mono
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Monospace, Sans Serif, Variable. The fixed-width sibling to Air Sans, with Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed stylistic sets.
summary: The fixed-width cut of Air by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo, with Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed stylistic sets.
description: The monospaced cut of Air by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo of Inari Type. Eighteen styles run from Thin to Black with italics, with stylistic sets labelled Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed for different code and editorial uses. Discretionary ligatures cover fi, si, sr, st, Th, plus alternate symbols, standard ligatures and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Pangram place Air Mono inside the Air collection, describing the family as a harmonious collision of brutalism, minimalism and aerospace originally designed by Mat Desjardins and expanded by Caio Kondo from Inari Type. They list Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed stylistic sets, with discretionary ligatures and case-sensitive forms.

name: Air Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The proportional sibling to Air Mono, sharing the same stylistic sets and alternates.
summary: The proportional sans cut of Air by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo, paired with Air Mono.
description: The proportional sans cut of Air by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo of Inari Type. Eighteen styles run from Thin to Black with italics and a variable axis. Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed stylistic sets carry across to Air Mono, so a project can pair the two cuts cleanly through code, captions and headlines.
foundry_blurb: Pangram pitch Air Sans alongside Air Mono inside the Air collection, describing the wider family as a harmonious collision of brutalism, minimalism and aerospace. They credit Mat Desjardins as the original designer with Caio Kondo from Inari Type expanding the system, with stylistic sets and alternate characters running across both widths.

name: Bitmap Font Pack
classification_notes: Both subfamilies are bitmap typefaces designed in a pixel-grid aesthetic, placing them firmly in the display category; they draw from classic grotesques and serifs but render them in a brutalist pixel idiom.
summary: A two-family bitmap collection by Steve Marchal, drawing from classic grotesques and serifs and reinterpreting them through a pixel-grid, brutalist aesthetic.
description: The pack comprises two subfamilies, NeueBit and Mondwest, each available in Regular and Bold weights with Japanese Kana support and over 600 glyphs per cut. Both faces translate familiar grotesque and serif references into a precise pixel format, pushed toward a more futuristic and brutalist visual register than their historical sources. Pangram Pangram position the pack as a pairing companion to their own Neue Montreal and Editorial New.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe the Bitmap Font Pack as pixel-perfect nostalgia, designed by Steve Marchal as a go-to pixel font inspired by classic grotesques and serifs. With over 600 characters, symbols and icons per cut, the pack was crafted for legibility, versatility and pairing capacity, pushing digitalisation further into a futuristic and brutalist aesthetic.

name: Casa Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil display typeface with serif characteristics, soft curves and deliberate erosion effects. Categorised as display due to its explicit positioning for titles and logos rather than running text.
summary: A single-weight stencil serif by Mat Desjardins, distinguished by soft curves and a textured, eroded quality suited to display work.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins and updated in 2018, Casa Stencil offers one Regular weight with 237 glyphs and broad Latin language support. The stencil cuts and eroded details give letterforms a worn, tactile quality that sits well in title settings and logo work. Coverage extends across Western and Central European languages including Russian and Serbian.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Casa Stencil as a beautiful, elegant stencil font featuring soft curves and a sense of erosion, designed for title and logo applications. Commercial licences include eight digital flower posters, and the font is available in OTF, TTF, WOFF and WOFF2 formats.

name: Charlevoix Pro
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as Grotesk and Sans Serif, but the slightly rounded corners, large display intent and regional inspiration push it firmly into display territory. Its structure is geometric with humanist softening rather than a workhorse grotesque.
summary: A display sans with slightly rounded corners, drawing loose inspiration from the Charlevoix region of Quebec for use in headlines and brand work.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins and released in 2015, it comes in nine weights from Thin to Black, each with 325 glyphs covering a broad range of Latin-script languages. The rounded terminals soften an otherwise sturdy grotesque skeleton, making it more approachable at large sizes without losing structural clarity. A practical choice for editorial titles, posters and brand identities where a headline face with some warmth is needed.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Charlevoix Pro as a display typeface inspired by one of Quebec's most iconic regions, with its strength and boldness reflecting the hills and mountains of Charlevoix and its fluid qualities echoing the Saint-Laurent River. Slightly rounded corners give the font a human touch when set in titles.

name: Chronos Serif
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry under Display, Expressive, and Serif. The three variants — Stroked, Hatched, and Filled — are decorative treatments that place this firmly in display territory rather than conventional text serif use.
summary: A decorative display serif in three stylistically distinct variants — Stroked, Hatched and Filled — designed for high-end visual work.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins, Chronos Serif offers three treatments of the same letterforms: an outline Stroked style, a crosshatched Hatched style, and a solid Filled style. The elaborately curved forms and textural detail make it suited to luxury branding, packaging and editorial headlines where visual weight and ornamentation are the point. Language support covers a broad range of Latin-based scripts including Central and Eastern European languages.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Chronos Serif as a refined display serif that brings elegance, texture, and character into every letterform. Designed with versatility and high-end aesthetics in mind, it offers three distinct variants — Stroked, Hatched and Filled — each with its own unique visual language and loaded with detail and elaborate curves.

name: Cirka
classification_notes: Vertical-axis construction with high contrast and sharply pointed, flamboyant serifs distinguish Cirka from conventional transitional designs, pushing it toward expressive display territory while retaining traditional letterform proportions.
summary: A vertical-axis serif with sharply pointed, flamboyant serifs drawn by Nick Losacco, citing the geometric drama of Italian supercar design as its reference point.
description: Five weights from Light to Bold, available as a variable font, with classic upright construction undercut by razor-sharp terminals and pointed serifs that give headlines a distinct edge. The contrast between restrained letterform proportions and those aggressive serifs makes it suited to fashion branding, luxury packaging and editorial work where elegance with bite is needed. Glyph coverage extends across Latin Extended, supporting over 30 languages including Czech, Polish and Romanian.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Cirka as what happens when high fashion meets high velocity — a serif with sharp ambition combining classic vertical-axis construction with unexpectedly pointed, flamboyant serifs. Inspired by the Italian Wedge design language of vintage supercars, they position it as equally at home on minimalist posters and refined editorial layouts, neither shouting nor whispering, but gliding.

name: Editorial New
classification_notes: A narrow serif with moderate contrast and curvaceous italics, designed primarily for long-form editorial use. Its proportions and contrast lean toward a transitional or contemporary editorial serif idiom.
summary: A narrow, high-contrast serif by Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, built for long-form editorial layouts with enough personality to hold its own as a display face.
description: Sixteen styles span eight weights from Thin to Heavy, each with a matching italic, and the family ships as a variable font. The narrow proportions suit column-based editorial work, while the heavier weights and expressive italics lend themselves to headlines and pull quotes. Its mid-1990s editorial reference sits comfortably in fashion, beauty and lifestyle publishing contexts.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Editorial New as a precise and elegant narrow serif designed with long-form content in mind, though they note it carries enough flair to double as a display option. They position it as evoking a mid-90s editorial aesthetic, referencing high-end fashion magazines and luxury brand identities, while pointing to the regular weight as a workhorse for immersive reading and the heavier weights as tools for bold typographic statements.

name: Editorial Old
designer: Mat Desjardins, Fred Wiltshire, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: A narrow serif with softened curves, relaxed connections and droopy ligatures; the foundry describes it as an eroded, vintage-inflected spin-off of Editorial New, placing it within a transitional serif tradition with display-leaning personality.
summary: A narrow serif with softened curves and abundant ligatures, designed as a looser, more eroded companion to Editorial New with a mid-90s retro character.
description: Born as a deliberate reworking of Pangram Pangram's Editorial New, this typeface relaxes the tension in the curves, softens the terminals and introduces droopy, luscious ligatures that lean into an eroded, vintage sensibility. Sixteen styles span eight weights from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, and the family is available as a variable font. At 463 glyphs per style, it covers a broad range of Latin-based languages and includes OpenType features such as discretionary ligatures in both upper and lower case, standard ligatures and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Editorial Old as a fresh take on their popular Editorial New, pushing the vintage concept further by relaxing curves, softening terminals and adding droopy ligatures to create what they call a melting, eroded version of the original. The concept is rooted in mid-90s retro aesthetics while remaining contemporary, with visuals directed by Two Times Elliott.

name: Editorial Sans
designer: Andrea Biggio, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram Pangram classify it as Humanist Sans Serif. It shares proportions and metrics with the serif Editorial New, giving it warm, text-friendly characteristics typical of humanist sans designs.
summary: A humanist sans companion to Editorial New, sharing its proportions and metrics across 16 styles with true italics and variable axes.
description: Designed by Andrea Biggio, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, it takes the skeleton of the serif Editorial New and strips away the serifs while preserving the underlying spacing and proportions. Eight weights from Thin to Heavy are paired with matching true italics, a feature the foundry notes is uncommon in sans-serif releases. Available as variable fonts alongside static OTF and TTF, it suits editorial layouts, branding and screen work where a warmer grotesque character is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Editorial Sans as a typeface that bridges the past and present with a retro-modern aesthetic, building on the legacy of Editorial New by retaining the same proportions and metrics in a sleek sans-serif form. They position it across editorial layouts, branding, web design and advertising, noting its 16 meticulously crafted styles with true italics as a rare feature in sans-serif type.

name: Eiko
classification_notes: A high-contrast serif with Mincho-influenced structure, combining sharp hairline strokes with strong verticals in a manner consistent with Japanese Mincho traditions filtered through a contemporary editorial lens.
summary: A high-contrast serif by Caio Kondo that draws on Japanese Mincho forms and is named in honour of art director Eiko Ishioka, with Latin and kana coverage across 16 styles.
description: Designed by Caio Kondo, it pairs sharp hairline strokes with strong verticals drawn from Mincho tradition, while the Latin forms sit comfortably in editorial and luxury branding contexts. The family covers hiragana, katakana and 81 educational kanji, making it one of the few commercial serifs designed to pair with existing kanji fonts rather than replace them. Features include vertical writing support, old-style figures, stylistic alternates, ligatures and a full italic suite added in version 2.00.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Eiko as a tribute to Eiko Ishioka, the Japanese art director known for bold, genre-defying work. They position it as a high-contrast serif that takes cues from traditional Mincho forms while bringing modern editorial sophistication, and highlight its rare ability to blend Latin and Japanese typography in multilingual designs. The family includes kana alphabets, 81 educational kanji, vertical writing support, and an expansive feature set of alternates, ligatures and symbolic characters.

name: Font Starter Pack
classification_notes: This is not a single typeface but a bundled subscription pack of 79 typefaces across multiple categories; no single classification applies, so the dominant Pangram Pangram catalogue style of grotesque and contemporary sans-serif is used as a proxy.
summary: A subscription bundle giving access to 79 typefaces from Pangram Pangram and Off Type, with over 1,250 styles and 42 Photoshop mockups, priced from $29 per year.
description: Three tiered annual plans cover between one and ten commercial projects per year, with all 79 typefaces available across every tier. The bundle includes fonts from both the Pangram Pangram and Off Type catalogues in OTF, TTF, WOFF and WOFF2 formats, alongside 42 Photoshop mockups made in collaboration with Bendito and Layers. Subscribers receive early access to new releases and free updates for the duration of their subscription.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram position the Font Starter Pack as the fastest way to upgrade a designer's type library, offering 79 typefaces, 1,250+ key styles, all formats, and 42 pro mockups under commercial licences starting at $29 per year, with free lifetime updates and early access to future releases.

name: Formula
classification_notes: Pangram Pangram classify Formula as Grotesk and Sans Serif; it spans six widths from Condensed to Extended with a racing-inspired character and grotesque structure.
summary: A wide-ranging grotesque from Mat Desjardins spanning six widths and nine weights, with italics and variable cuts throughout, aimed at campaign and branding work.
description: Formula runs from Condensed to Extended across 108 static styles, each width offering nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, plus variable font cuts. The design leans on a bold, kinetic character suited to motorsport-adjacent branding and campaign work, while lighter weights in the Normal width are serviceable for body text. Hugo Jourdan contributed updates in version 2.0, released March 2022.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Formula as racy by nature and grotesk by design, built to make an impactful statement whether deployed as a Condensed Black Italic for a race-inspired campaign or a Normal Regular for body text. It ships in six sub-families each with 18 styles plus a variable cut, covering 685 glyphs per style and broad Latin language support.

name: Fragment
classification_notes: Fragment spans four distinct subfamilies including a high-contrast serif, a mid-serif called Glare, a grotesque sans and a text cut, all rooted in 19th-century lettering traditions. The display-oriented orientation and vintage lettering revival make 'display' the most accurate primary category, though it functions across multiple genre boundaries.
summary: A four-family type system drawing on 19th-century lettering and signs, pairing a grotesque sans, a high-contrast serif, a mid-serif called Glare and a text cut across 64 styles plus variable cuts.
description: Designed by Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins, Fragment ties four distinct but complementary subfamilies into a single collection: a strong sans, a high-contrast serif, an intermediate mid-serif named Glare, and a text-optimised cut. Each family runs to 18 styles with italics and a variable axis, and every weight ships 581 glyphs with alternates. The vintage lettering reference gives the system a consistent historical character that prevents the four voices from feeling arbitrary.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Fragment as their 35th release, born from vintage lettering and signs and bridging 19th-century letterforms with contemporary typography. The collection uses the latest variable font technology to move gracefully between an elegant high-contrast serif, a revival mid-serif called Glare, and a strong sans, each with distinct personalities yet perfectly complementary, with 32 unique weights plus italics and 581 glyphs per style.

name: Fraktion
classification_notes: Pangram Pangram categorise it as Grotesk; it draws from vintage signage lettering but resolves into a contemporary grotesque with squared-off rounds, compact capitals and ink traps, accompanied by a fixed-width companion.
summary: A contemporary grotesque by Juri Zaech with a slight industrial edge, paired with a fixed-width companion that trades technicality for playfulness.
description: Fraktion Sans takes loose inspiration from vintage signage lettering but lands somewhere more neutral, retaining only the squared-off rounds and compact capitals as visible traces of that origin. An exaggerated spur on the lowercase 'a', ink traps and true verticals on rounded strokes give it enough character for branding while keeping it usable for screen and editorial work. Fraktion Mono refits the letterforms for a fixed-width grid and ends up the more informal of the two, both sets offered across seven weights with italic counterparts and variable font cuts.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Fraktion as structured to misbehave, a contemporary grotesque exploring the tension between mechanical sturdiness and expressive clarity. Inspired by vintage signage lettering but departing far from it, the family pairs a proportional sans with a monospaced companion, each offering seven upright weights, italic counterparts, variable fonts and OpenType features including stylistic alternates, case-sensitive punctuation, fractions and a slashed zero.

name: Frama
designer: Samuel Salminen, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Explicitly rooted in 20th-century modernism with Futura and Avant Garde as reference points; letterforms built from circles, straight lines and considered counterforms in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans serif in Display and Text cuts, drawing on Futura and Avant Garde while introducing optical warmth through subtle irregularities.
description: Designed by Samuel Salminen, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, Frama is built from circles, straight lines and carefully weighted counterforms that recall early 20th-century modernism without copying it. Nine weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics in both the Display and Text subfamilies, and a variable font covers the full range. Stylistic alternates for characters including 'a', 'g', 'M' and punctuation give designers room to shift tone without breaking the family's underlying calm.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Frama as a contemporary geometric sans rooted in early modernism, citing Futura's structural clarity and Avant Garde's expressive geometry as its primary references. They present it as a typeface where simple geometric principles — circles, straight lines and counterforms — work together in a restrained system, with subtle optical adjustments adding warmth. Available in 28 styles across Display and Text cuts, with a variable font and extensive stylistic alternates, it is positioned for editorial, branding, interface and motion design work.

name: Fuji Sans
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a grotesk sans-serif; slightly condensed proportions and structured curves place it firmly in the grotesque tradition with geometric influences.
summary: A slightly condensed grotesque sans in four weights, drawing on Japanese design principles of restraint and structural clarity.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins and named after Mount Fuji, the typeface takes slightly condensed proportions and structured curves as its formal premise. Four weights run from Light to Bold with variable font support, covering Latin Extended character sets across more than 30 languages. Suited to brand identities, editorial headlines and digital interfaces where a clean, controlled grotesque is needed without obvious personality quirks.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Fuji Sans as a bold sans-serif grotesk inspired by the quiet strength and refined beauty of Japanese design heritage. They note its slightly condensed proportions and structured curves make it well suited to brand identities, editorial headlines, posters and digital interfaces, positioning it as versatile enough for logotypes, UX and packaging alike.

name: Gatwick
classification_notes: Pangram categorise Gatwick as Display, Expressive, Humanist and Sans Serif. Its defining characteristics — tapered stems, sharp terminals and high-contrast optical size variants — place it squarely in the incise display tradition with a humanist sensibility.
summary: A wide incise display family by Valerio Monopoli, distinguished by tapered stems and sharp terminals across three optical subfamilies — Glider, Jet and Jumbo.
description: Gatwick spans 24 styles in three subfamilies, each available as a variable cut, running from Hairline to Bold. The Glider subfamily sits at lower contrast; Jet and Jumbo introduce increasing high contrast, intended for elegant headlines and subheadings. Tapered stems and sharp terminals set it apart from more neutral wide grotesques, giving the family a distinctly extravagant character suited to branding and packaging.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Gatwick as a wide incise typeface that blends a funky attitude with a refined spirit, appealing equally to vintage and contemporary aesthetics. Version 2.0 introduced two high-contrast subfamilies — Jet and Jumbo — alongside the original Glider, bringing the total to 24 styles with 691 glyphs each, all available in variable format.

name: Gosha Sans
classification_notes: Geometric construction with hard corners and strong vertical strokes drawn from Soviet-era and Eastern European typographic traditions; the foundry categorises it as Brutalist and Geometric Sans.
summary: A geometric sans with hard corners and a brutalist edge, drawn from vintage Eastern European and Soviet-era typographic sources.
description: Eight weights from Thin to Ultrabold, available as a variable font, give the family a practical range across headlines, logos and UI work. Alternate characters for A, S and U let designers push the Soviet reference further or dial it back towards a cleaner grotesk. Cyrillic support sits alongside a broad Latin Extended character set covering more than thirty languages.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Gosha Sans as a strong and versatile sans inspired by Eastern European and Russian vintage design trends. They highlight its hard corners and strong lines as sources of personality without compromising legibility at small sizes, and encourage designers to explore the alternate characters for a more pronounced brutalist effect.

name: Grafier
classification_notes: Grafier takes Baskerville as its structural starting point but replaces classical curved serifs with uncompromisingly straight, flat serifs and flag-terminals, giving it a brutalist edge that sits between transitional serif tradition and contemporary architectural letterform design.
summary: A Baskerville-derived serif by Alex Slobzheninov that replaces curved serifs with hard, flat cuts and flag-terminals for a blunt, architectural effect.
description: Starting from the proportions and weight contrast of 18th-century Baskerville, Grafier strips out the calligraphic softness and replaces it with straight serifs that join letterforms with no curves, alongside chopped terminals and squarish punctuation. The family runs across five weights in both Text and Display contrast variants, with a variable font spanning weight and contrast axes. Cyrillic support broadens its reach, and stylistic alternates that disconnect serifs offer a usable option at smaller sizes.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Grafier as old-school form with a new-school attitude, inspired by Baskerville but reengineered for a sharper, digital-first age. Its signature feature is uncompromisingly straight serifs with clean joins and flag-terminals, offered in ten styles across five weights and two contrast variations, plus a variable font with weight and contrast axes. They position it for modern branding, editorial systems and UI work where straight-sided letterforms pair naturally with sans-serifs.

name: Hatton
classification_notes: Pangram Pangram classify Hatton explicitly as a humanist serif; its forms are drawn from the hand-painted signage and ornate shopfronts of London's Hatton Garden, lending organic warmth and refined imperfection to the letterforms.
summary: A humanist serif drawn from the weathered signage and shopfronts of London's Hatton Garden jewellery quarter, available in nine weights with true italics and variable font support.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins in collaboration with London studio Two Times Elliott and later extended by type designer Morgane Vantorre, Hatton takes its forms from the hand-painted signs and ornate facades of Clerkenwell and Hatton Garden. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and includes a variable cut alongside a rich set of OpenType features: vintage alternates, discretionary ligatures, old-style figures and a diamond-shaped eight. Its proportions and adjusted contrast suit branding, packaging and editorial work where a sense of craft and quiet opulence is required.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Hatton as a typographic homage to Hatton Garden, London's infamous jewellery quarter, initially designed in partnership with Two Times Elliott and later updated by type designer Morgane Vantorre. They say the typeface distils the distinctive character of the district's street signage, shopfronts and landmarks, including their hand-rendered imperfections, into a variable humanist serif spanning eight well-to-do styles from Ultralight to Black, now expanded with true italics, refined curves, adjusted contrast and a new set of glyphs.

name: Kyoto
designer: Caio Kondo, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: A slab serif with calligraphic influences drawn from Japanese writing systems, featuring strong bracketed slabs paired with soft teardrop terminals; classified as display due to its expressive character and headline orientation.
summary: A display slab serif with nine weights and full Latin and Japanese kana support, shaped by a conceptual study of hiragana and katakana forms.
description: Bold slab strokes sit alongside soft teardrop terminals, giving the face a rhythmic tension between structure and fluency. The design originated as an exploration of how a Latin slab serif might visually echo Japanese writing systems, and that premise informs its distinctive calligraphic undercurrent. With 692 glyphs per style, hiragana, katakana, Japanese punctuation, and stylistic alternates including an alternate Q, it is well-equipped for bilingual editorial and international branding work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe PP Kyoto as a bold and expressive slab serif that bridges traditional influence with modern clarity, originally conceived as a typographic study of Japanese writing systems. The face pairs strong slabs with soft teardrop terminals and prominent dots to create a rhythmic visual flow, and includes complete hiragana and katakana support alongside a full Latin character set across 18 styles and a variable cut.

name: Lettra Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with explicit serif construction, drawing from typewriter aesthetics with strong vertical contrast and diamond-shaped details. Classified as mono due to fixed-width spacing being the defining structural constraint of the design.
summary: A monospaced serif by Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins that takes typewriter aesthetics as its starting point, adding strong vertical contrast and diamond-shaped details across eight weights with matching italics.
description: Each character is individually crafted to maintain consistent weight and proportion within a fixed-width grid, a constraint the designers treat as a design problem rather than a limitation. The typeface draws on classic serif typewriter forms and botanical illustration detail, with the latter informing its distinctive diamond terminals and high contrast strokes. An experimental character set includes unconventional ligatures, a double-width Oo and a sigil, alongside a comprehensive range of OpenType alternates.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Lettra Mono as a monospaced serif that explores the design challenges of modular spacing, with each character individually crafted for consistent weight and proportion. The design draws inspiration from classic serif typewriter fonts and botanical drawing classifications, featuring strong vertical contrast and distinctive diamond-shaped details. Eight weights in both upright and italic styles are supported, along with an experimental character set including unconventional ligatures, ordinal abbreviations and a double-width Oo.

name: Migra
classification_notes: Pangram categorise it as Display and Expressive; the sharp, spiky serifs and high-contrast sculptural forms are conceived for large-scale use rather than continuous text.
summary: A spiky display serif by Valerio Monopoli, its sharp terminals and fine cuts drawing on the aerodynamic forms of migratory birds.
description: Sixteen styles span eight weights from Extralight to Black, each with a matching italic that grows increasingly gestural as the weight increases. Sharp serifs and fine terminals give individual letterforms a taut, alert quality suited to fashion, art direction and high-end publishing. A variable font is included, and OpenType features cover discretionary ligatures, small caps, old-style figures and alternate characters for J, g and y.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Migra as a sharp, sculptural typeface capturing the tension and beauty of the natural world, specifically the aerodynamic elegance of migratory birds. They position featherlight weights as evoking high-altitude gliding and the Black as striking like a hawk mid-dive, and recommend it for fashion, art direction and high-end publishing where a design needs to feel curated and commanding.

name: Model
designer: Caio Kondo, Francesca Bolognini, Mat Desjardins
classification_notes: The typeface is described as geometric and modular in construction, with mechanical aesthetics drawn from plastic model kit components. It is classified as sans-serif with a geometric subcategory given its clean, grid-like forms.
summary: A 72-font geometric sans collection inspired by Tamiya plastic model kits, spanning four subfamilies from standard sans to experimental connected letterforms.
description: Designed by Caio Kondo, Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins, the collection draws its modular, mechanical aesthetic from the assembled components of Japanese scale model kits. The four subfamilies — Sans, Mono, Plastic and Plastic Line — each interpret the source material at a different level of abstraction, with Plastic and Plastic Line styles emulating the sprued parts before assembly. Alongside extended Latin coverage, the family includes Hiragana and Katakana scripts, tying the design concept back to its Japanese reference point.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Model as a tribute to plastic model kit culture, taking the iconic Japanese brand Tamiya as its primary reference. The design blends nostalgia with modern precision, channelling the assembly process through clean, modular forms and a mechanical aesthetic. The collection totals 72 fonts across four subfamilies, each available in upright and italic styles with variable cuts, and includes Hiragana, Katakana, Japanese punctuation and rare measurement characters alongside broad Latin language support.

name: Monument
classification_notes: A grotesk sans-serif with brutalist architectural influence, designed for display and titling use across five widths from condensed to extended.
summary: A brutalist-inspired grotesk spanning five widths and nine weights, each available as variable fonts with italics.
description: Drawing on the imposing forms of brutalist architecture, Monument is a display-leaning grotesk built for headlines and identity work. Five width variants run from Condensed to Extended, each offering nine weights in upright and italic styles, all as variable fonts. A major 2025 update expanded the family to 90 styles total, giving designers considerable range across screens and print.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Monument as a powerful grotesk typeface inspired by the raw beauty and imposing forms of brutalist architecture, originally designed to make bold statements and now a go-to for attention-grabbing titles and impactful design. With its 2025 update it has grown into one of their most expansive families, comprising five distinct widths each in nine weights across upright and italic styles, all available as variable fonts.

name: Mori
classification_notes: Categorised by the foundry as Gothic and Grotesk, Mori draws on contemporary Japanese design sensibilities while functioning as a workhorse grotesque, with subtle geometric details in characters like the 'r', 'G' and figures 6 and 9.
summary: A gothic grotesque by Caio Kondo, drawn from contemporary Japanese design with distinctive geometric details in selected characters across 22 styles and a variable axis.
description: Mori operates as a workhorse grotesque with quiet character: each of its 22 styles contains 804 glyphs including circled and squared numbers, Japanese punctuation, and multiple alternates for 'a', 'g', 'r', 't' and 'y'. The design is calm in most weights but distinctly detailed in characters such as the capital G, numerals 6 and 9, and the geometric single-storey 'r'. Available as a variable font with full italic coverage, it suits editorial, graphic design and brand identity work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Mori as a versatile and sophisticated gothic sans serif inspired by contemporary Japanese design, suited to editorial, graphic design and branding. Designed by Caio Kondo with collaborators Mat Desjardins and Satsuki Arakaki, it spans 22 styles from Thin to Black with 804 glyphs each, a variable font version, and OpenType features including alternate characters, discretionary ligatures, tabular figures and Japanese-influenced symbols.

name: Museum
designer: Andrea Biggio, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: A humanist sans-serif with subtle terminal flares drawn from Optima's glyphic tradition, placing it firmly in the humanist category rather than a neutral or geometric grotesque.
summary: A humanist sans with terminal flares drawn from Optima's glyphic heritage, spanning nine weights with true italics featuring swashes and decorative terminals.
description: Designed by Andrea Biggio, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, Museum takes Optima as its conceptual starting point, filtering that glyphic quality through research into vintage book covers, spines and museum typography. The terminal flares are most pronounced in the lighter weights and recede as the weight increases, keeping the family usable across body text and display settings. All nine weights include true italics with swashes and decorative terminals, an uncommon offering for a sans-serif family.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Museum as drawing inspiration from the meticulous sketches of Optima found on aged banknotes, reinterpreted with modern sensibility. The foundry highlight its subtle terminal flares as a glyphic quality rarely seen in contemporary sans-serif design, and note that its conceptual foundation was informed by research into vintage tomes, book covers and museum objects. The tapered diagonals are cited as adding dynamic movement, while the 18 styles and variable cut make it adaptable from refined body text to commanding headlines.

name: Neue Corp
designer: Maksym Kobuzan, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram Pangram categorise it as Grotesk and Geometric Sans Serif; it draws from neo-grotesque references including Univers and Helvetica Extended, while the condensed and black styles reference Hamilton wood type, placing it firmly in the grotesque tradition.
summary: A large grotesque family spanning eight widths and 20 weights each, drawing from Univers, Helvetica Extended and Hamilton wood type to cover everything from compressed headlines to wide body text.
description: Designed by Maksym Kobuzan, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, Neue Corp runs to 160 static styles plus variable cuts across eight width subfamilies from Compressed to Extended. Black and condensed cuts reference Hamilton Mfg. Co. wood type, wide bold styles lean on Helvetica Extended, and the regular weights soften Univers with friendlier details. Alternate stylistic sets shift the character between geometric neutrality and a spurred grotesque personality, and all subfamilies include Cyrillic support.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Corp as a versatile creative tool for advertising, marketing and digital work, built with a broad range of widths and weights to suit any context from bold headlines to body text. They position it as balancing character with neutrality, combining the eccentricity of old wood type with the precision of neo-grotesques, with stylistic sets that allow designers to dial between geometric neutrality and bold grotesque personality.

name: Neue Gstaad
designer: Ian Patry, Francesca Bolognini, Mat Desjardins
classification_notes: Categorised by the foundry as Grotesk and Sans Serif; the design draws on Swiss grotesque tradition with contemporary refinement, structured as a large variable system spanning compressed to expanded widths.
summary: A Swiss-inflected grotesque by Ian Patry spanning seven widths from Compressed to Expanded, built as a 112-style variable system.
description: Designed by Ian Patry with Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins, Neue Gstaad organises 16 styles per subfamily across seven width variants, yielding 112 styles in total alongside variable font cuts for each. The design references classic Swiss grotesque sensibilities while seeking a quieter, more restrained expression rather than overt historicism. Broad language support covers Latin Extended and Cyrillic scripts, and the family is built with OpenType features throughout.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Gstaad as a contemporary type family shaped by a distinctly Swiss sense of refinement, calm, confidence and precision. Designed by Ian Patry, it moves beyond regional clichés toward a broader, more universal expression of Swiss design rooted in clarity, discretion and quiet sophistication. The family spans 7 subfamilies and 112 styles, built as a complete variable system from Compressed to Expanded, supporting a wide range of Latin-based and Cyrillic languages.

name: Neue Machina Collection
classification_notes: Described as monospace/geometric in character, with a grotesk structure and ink trap details in heavier weights, placing it firmly in the geometric sans category with display tendencies.
summary: A geometric grotesk in two cuts — Inktrap and Plain — designed around machine aesthetics, with prominent ink traps emerging at heavier weights.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins and Valerio Monopoli, Neue Machina draws on robotics and digital-age aesthetics to produce a typeface that shifts character across its weight range. Lighter settings read as a clean geometric sans; heavier weights reveal deep ink traps that give the family a distinctly mechanical personality. Each of the two cuts — Inktrap and Plain — ships with nine upright styles, nine italics and a variable font, with Cyrillic support across both.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Machina as a love letter to the digital age, a powerful geometric typeface inspired by the aesthetics of robotics and machines. They note its versatility: lighter weights blend into layouts cleanly, while heavier weights carry strong personality through their ink traps. The collection comprises two sub-families — Inktrap and Plain — each with 18 styles plus a variable cut, full italic support and Cyrillic coverage.

name: Neue Montreal
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque sans-serif with roots in modernist typographic tradition, self-described as a grotesque and structured around clean, rational proportions typical of the genre.
summary: A contemporary grotesque by Mat Desjardins, first released in 2018 and developed continuously into a 36-style family covering Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Arabic.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins and inspired loosely by Montreal's modernist visual culture, the family spans Hairline to Black with matching italics across a variable weight axis. Version 3 added a dedicated Text subfamily with wider apertures and adjusted spacing for improved legibility at small sizes. Language support covers over 500 languages across Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic and Vietnamese scripts.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Montreal as a contemporary grotesque built over time rather than designed once, evolving since its 2018 release into a modern classic trusted across branding, editorial and digital design. Inspired by Montreal's design heritage from Expo 67 onwards, it has grown from a modest weight range into a comprehensive system spanning Hairline to Black with a dedicated Text variant, and now supports over 500 languages and 3.4 billion speakers across Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Arabic scripts.

name: Neue Montreal Arabic
classification_notes: A modern Arabic grotesque designed as a companion to Neue Montreal, sharing the clean, neutral grotesque character of the Latin original while respecting the structural logic of Arabic script.
summary: An Arabic grotesque companion to Neue Montreal, built across seven weights with Cyrillic support and a variable cut for multilingual design work.
description: Developed with Studio Safar as collaborator, Neue Montreal Arabic extends Pangram Pangram's popular grotesque into Arabic script with equal attention paid to weight matching, baseline alignment and typographic tone. Seven weights run from Thin to Bold, each carrying 879 glyphs covering Arabic, Cyrillic and extended Latin, with a variable font also available. The family is positioned for brand identity, web interfaces and editorial contexts where Latin and Arabic text must sit together without either script feeling secondary.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Montreal Arabic as a contemporary Arabic typeface designed to bring visual harmony to multilingual design systems. They emphasise that unlike many Arabic extensions that feel like afterthoughts, this family was developed with equal care to the Latin original, with close attention to baseline alignment, weight matching and typographic tone. Available in seven weights with a variable cut and optimised for both print and screen, they position it as a typographic bridge for design systems that value clarity and cross-language coherence.

name: Neue Montreal Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width monospace typeface derived from Neue Montreal, a grotesque sans-serif, retaining its clean geometric-grotesque character while adding coding-specific features and ligatures.
summary: A monospace companion to Neue Montreal with coding ligatures, a variable axis and Cyrillic support across 14 styles.
description: Built as a fixed-width counterpart to Pangram Pangram's popular Neue Montreal, this family spans seven weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic, and ships as a variable font. Coding ligatures were contributed by Fred Wiltshire, with additional features including a slashed zero, round dot alternates, boxed glyphs and case-sensitive forms. Cyrillic coverage extends the family to Belarusian, Bulgarian, Russian, Serbian, Ukrainian and more.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Montreal Mono as a fusion of style and functionality, introducing coding ligatures and a special button mode alongside a clean, modern aesthetic. The family offers 14 styles with 2,339 glyphs each, Cyrillic support and multilingual coverage, designed for both designers and developers.

name: Neue World
classification_notes: Described as a 'super-serif' with high contrast between thick and thin strokes in heavier weights and soft curves in lighter ones, oriented primarily toward display and headline use across a wide range of widths.
summary: A high-contrast display serif spanning six widths and 48 styles, with variable font support and Cyrillic coverage, designed for headline and poster work.
description: Inspired by vintage display types that were manually condensed or expanded for print, Neue World offers six width variants from Super Condensed to Extended, each in eight weights running from Thin to Black. Soft curves keep the lighter cuts refined, while heavy weights lean into bold contrast for commanding display use. Variable font cuts are included across all six sub-families, and the full character set covers a broad range of Latin and Cyrillic languages.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue World as a free-to-try super-serif family with 6 widths and 48 cuts, inspired by vintage display types once manually squeezed to fit print formats. They note that its soft curves and refined details bring elegance to the lighter weights, while hard contrast in heavier weights delivers bold statements, calling it a modern serif that pays homage to its forerunners.

name: Neue York
designer: Andrea Biggio, Francesca Bolognini, Mat Desjardins
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as a gothic sans serif, drawing explicitly on American gothic lettering traditions and New York City visual culture, with a structured yet slightly rough character consistent with that lineage.
summary: A 48-style gothic sans in three widths, drawn from New York City's street-level visual culture and American gothic lettering traditions.
description: Designed by Andrea Biggio, Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins in collaboration with Collins New York, Neue York pulls from bodega signage, subway mosaics and worn American gothics to build a contemporary type system with a deliberately vintage edge. Eight weights from Thin to Black, each with an italic, run across Condensed, Narrow and Normal widths, with differing x-heights tuned to different roles: the Condensed sits taller for headlines, the Normal proportioned for text. Each sub-family also ships with a variable cut.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue York as shaped by the city's layered visual noise, citing bodega awnings, worn American gothics, mosaic station tiles and the rhythm of Avenues and Streets. The goal was a contemporary type system that carries a vintage pulse: clean in structure and functional in spirit, but gently rough around the edges, like something that has lived out in the open.

name: Nikkei
designer: Caio Kondo, Francesca Bolognini, Mat Desjardins
classification_notes: The foundry categorises it as Geometric and Grotesk; four sub-families share a sans-serif structure with geometric underpinnings drawn from Japanese-American cultural sources including hand-lettered signage and community newspapers.
summary: A four-subfamily sans-serif collection built as a typographic tribute to Japanese immigration to America, drawing on hand-lettered ships' signage, Nikkei community newspapers and Japanese family crests.
description: Designed by Caio Kondo, Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins, the collection comprises four distinct families — Maru, Journal, Line and Pacific — each with 18 styles plus a variable cut and a Crest Set. The subfamilies share a geometric grotesque foundation while each expressing a different formal register, from the rounded warmth of Maru to the structured clarity of Line. Italics and stylistic alternates are included throughout, making it suitable for title compositions with varied rhythmic options.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe PP Nikkei as a typeface woven with memory — a tribute to Japanese immigrants and their descendants living outside Japan, the term 'Nikkei' itself referring to that diaspora. Inspiration was drawn from hand-lettering found on ships, community newspapers, and Japanese family crests, resulting in five typographic systems (four released as sub-families) that share a historical narrative while each expressing distinct concepts. The collection offers versatile title fonts with proportions and stylistic alternatives suited to playful title compositions.

name: Object Sans
classification_notes: Object Sans blends Swiss neo-grotesk neutrality with geometric construction, placing it firmly in the geometric grotesque subcategory. The foundry explicitly references both Swiss neo-grotesks and geometric fonts as its dual influences.
summary: A geometric grotesque with Swiss neo-grotesk roots, designed to cover branding, UI and editorial work across a single family of 16 styles.
description: Drawn by Alex Slobzheninov, Object Sans combines the clean neutrality of Swiss grotesques with geometric structure, producing a family that sits comfortably across logo design, screen UI, packaging and body text. The Regular weight has been tuned specifically for small-size legibility, while the heavier styles hold up in large display settings. The family ships as a variable font with Latin Extended and Cyrillic coverage, and includes an alternate single-storey 'a' and a numbers-to-arrows feature.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Object Sans as a contemporary type family combining the best qualities of Swiss neo-grotesks and geometric fonts. They position it as a multifunctional workhorse suited to logo design, brand identities, websites, packaging, posters and headlines, with the Regular weight carefully tuned for small-sized body text in both print and on-screen contexts.

name: PP Kyoto
classification_notes: Slab serif with bold slabs, soft teardrop terminals and prominent dots; began as a study of Japanese writing systems and ships with hiragana, katakana and Japanese punctuation alongside Latin.
summary: A bold slab serif with teardrop terminals and full hiragana and katakana support, built for headlines and bilingual editorial.
description: PP Kyoto is a slab serif with thick slabs, soft teardrop terminals and rhythmic dots that give headlines real bite. It runs across nine weights from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, eighteen styles plus a variable cut. The Latin set sits alongside complete hiragana, katakana and Japanese punctuation, with stylistic alternates including an alternate Q across 692 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe PP Kyoto as a slab serif that bridges tradition and modernity, designed to stand out in headlines through bold slabs, teardrop terminals and prominent dots. They trace its origin to a study of Japanese writing systems and frame it as a bilingual tool with full Latin, hiragana, katakana and Japanese punctuation across 692 glyphs. They highlight 9 weights with italics, stylistic alternates and an alternate Q for designers who want visual impact with technical consistency.

name: PP Lettra Mono
classification_notes: Monospaced serif with strong vertical contrast and diamond-shaped details; draws on serif typewriter fonts and botanical drawing classifications, with each glyph individually crafted for consistent weight on a fixed grid.
summary: A monospaced serif with strong vertical contrast and diamond details, drawing on typewriter fonts and botanical classifications.
description: PP Lettra Mono is a monospaced serif that explores modular spacing while keeping the bite of a high-contrast text serif. Eight weights run upright and italic across sixteen styles, with diamond-shaped details, alternate characters and an experimental set including unconventional ligatures, ordinals, double-width Oo and a sigil. Sits well in editorial systems and technical documents that want a typewriter mood without the period parody.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Lettra as a monospaced serif that explores the design challenges of modular spacing, with each character crafted to hold consistent weight and proportion on a fixed grid. They cite serif typewriter fonts and botanical drawing classifications as inspiration, and call out strong vertical contrast and diamond-shaped details. They flag eight weights with italics, wide language support, alternates and an experimental set with unusual ligatures, ordinal abbreviations, double-width Oo and a distinctive sigil.

name: PP Migra
classification_notes: High-contrast display serif with sharp, spiky terminals; weights span from a refined Extralight to a heavy Black, with gestural italics and a set of special ligatures.
summary: A spiky high-contrast display serif inspired by migratory birds, with eight weights and gestural italics for editorial and fashion work.
description: PP Migra is a sharp, high-contrast display serif inspired by the features of migratory birds. Eight weights run from an austere Extralight to a powerful Black, with sixteen total styles including italics and a variable font. The italic is more gestural than the upright and ships with special ligatures, which makes Migra a natural fit for fashion editorial, magazine covers and luxury identity work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Migra as a spiky serif inspired by the features of migratory birds, spanning an austere and elegant light cut to a hawkish and powerful black. They flag a set of even more gestural italics and sundry special ligatures, and pitch the family as a way to add sparkle and grace to a design.

name: PP Model
subcategory: modular technical sans with mono and plastic cuts
classification_notes: Technical sans inspired by Tamiya plastic model kits, with four sub-families: Model Sans, Model Mono, Model Plastic and Model Plastic Line, each in 18 styles upright and italic with a variable cut. Total of 72 fonts.
summary: A 72-font technical system inspired by Tamiya model kits, with Sans, Mono, Plastic and Plastic Line cuts and Japanese support.
description: PP Model is a technical sans family inspired by the world of plastic model kits and the precision of Tamiya assembly instructions. Four sub-families run side by side: Model Sans, Model Mono, Model Plastic and Model Plastic Line, each in 18 styles with italics and a variable cut, for 72 fonts in total. The character set covers extended Latin, hiragana, katakana, Japanese punctuation and rarely-included measurement marks, which suits product, industrial and tech-flavoured identity work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram say Model pays tribute to the world of plastic model kits and is rooted in the iconic Japanese brand Tamiya, celebrating the meticulous art of model-making through clean, modular forms. They describe four sub-families, from the default Model and monospaced variants to experimental Plastic styles, totalling 72 fonts in upright and italic. They flag extensive Latin support, Hiragana, Katakana, Japanese punctuation and measurement characters that are rarely found.

name: PP Monument
classification_notes: Grotesque sans inspired by brutalist architecture; 2025 update extends the family to five widths, each in nine weights, in upright and italic, totalling ninety styles plus a variable cut.
summary: A brutalist grotesque grown into a 90-style system across five widths and nine weights for branding, editorial and digital work.
description: PP Monument is a brutalist grotesque designed to make bold, structural statements. The 2025 update has grown it into a 90-style system: five widths from Condensed through Wide, each in nine weights, in upright and italic, with a variable cut. That range makes Monument flex from compressed posters to comfortably wide editorial settings without breaking voice across screens and scales.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Monument as a powerful grotesk inspired by the raw beauty and imposing forms of brutalist architecture, originally designed to make bold statements. They flag the major 2025 update as a step into one of their most expansive families, with five distinct widths, each in nine carefully tuned weights, across upright and italic styles. They pitch it as a workhorse with edge for branding systems, editorial layouts and digital experiences.

name: PP Mori
classification_notes: Gothic sans serif inspired by contemporary Japanese design; eleven weights upright and italic for twenty-two styles, each with 804 glyphs and OpenType features.
summary: A gothic sans inspired by contemporary Japanese design, with twenty-two styles, deep OpenType features and a quietly distinctive r and g.
description: PP Mori is a gothic sans serif influenced by contemporary Japanese graphic design, built to carry both editorial and branding workloads. Eleven weights run from Thin to Heavy with matching italics for twenty-two styles, and each font carries 804 glyphs with circled and squared numerals, sub and superscripts, Japanese-flavoured punctuation and a deep set of alternates. Look for the geometric r, the double-storey g and the slightly off G to spot it in the wild.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Mori as a versatile and sophisticated gothic sans serif inspired by contemporary Japanese design, best suited for editorial, graphic design and branding. They flag 22 styles from Thin to Black, each with 804 glyphs and OpenType features including circled and squared numbers, sub and superscript numbers, Japanese punctuation, mathematical symbols and many alternates. They invite designers to look at the geometric r, double-story g, G and numerals 6 and 9 as quiet points of difference.

name: PP Museum
classification_notes: Humanist sans-serif with subtle terminal flares that recede in heavier weights; references sketches of Optima on aged banknotes and vintage book and museum typography. Italics carry swashes and decorative terminals.
summary: A humanist sans with glyphic flares and swash italics, drawn from Optima sketches on banknotes and vintage book typography.
description: PP Museum is a humanist sans with glyphic terminal flares that thin out as weight builds, giving a stone-cut quality rare in contemporary sans. The reference points are Optima sketches found on aged banknotes and vintage book and museum-object typography, and the family runs nine weights with true italics carrying swashes and decorative terminals. It moves comfortably from refined body text to commanding headlines for cultural, editorial and luxury work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Museum as a humanist sans-serif drawn from meticulous sketches of Optima found on aged banknotes, reinterpreted with a modern sensibility. They flag subtle terminal flares that echo a glyphic quality rarely seen in contemporary type, and reference research into vintage tomes, book covers, spines and museum-centric objects as the conceptual base. They highlight 9 weights with true italics that carry swashes and decorative terminals, an uncommon feature in sans-serif typefaces.

name: PP Neue Corp
classification_notes: Advertising and corporate system across eight widths and ten weights for 160 styles; Black and Condensed cuts reference Hamilton Mfg. Co. wood type, Wide Bolds reference Helvetica Extended, regular styles draw on Univers with softened details. Stylistic sets toggle between geometric neutrality and grotesque with spurs.
summary: A 160-style advertising and corporate system across eight widths, with wood type, Helvetica Extended and Univers references and toggle sets for spurs.
description: PP Neue Corp is a sprawling advertising and corporate system designed as an all-purpose creative tool. It runs across eight widths from Compressed to Extended and ten weights for 160 styles, with stylistic sets that toggle between a more geometric neutral cut and a grotesque cut with spurs. The references are explicit: Black and Condensed styles draw on Hamilton wood type, the Wide Bolds nod to Helvetica Extended, and the regular weights soften Univers for a friendlier tone.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Corp as a versatile typeface crafted as the ultimate creative tool for advertising, marketing and digital work, balancing eccentricity with neo-grotesque precision. They cite Hamilton Mfg. Co. wood type behind the Black and Condensed cuts, Helvetica Extended behind the Wide Bolds, and Univers with softened details behind the regular styles. They highlight stylistic alternates that swing the family between geometric neutrality and a bolder grotesque character with added spurs.

name: PP Neue Machina
classification_notes: Geometric display sans with monospace features and pronounced ink traps in heavier weights; ships in two cuts, Inktrap and Plain, each with italics and a variable font, for 36 styles total.
summary: A geometric display sans with monospace features and deep ink traps, in Inktrap and Plain cuts for technology and futurist work.
description: PP Neue Machina is a geometric display sans with monospace features and pronounced ink traps in its heavier weights, drawn from the aesthetics of robotics and machinery. It ships in two cuts, an aggressive Inktrap and a calmer Plain, each with italics and a variable font for 36 styles in total. Lighter weights blend in for body and interface use, while the heavy Inktrap cut carries the load for tech-flavoured headlines and product identity.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Machina as a powerful, meticulously crafted typeface with monospace and geometric features and apparent, deep ink traps in its heavier weights, inspired by the aesthetics of robotics and machines. They pitch it as versatile, blending in at lighter weights or carrying personality at heavier ones, suited for the future of technology. They flag an Inktrap cut and a Plain cut for more subtle work, each with italics and full variable support.

name: PP Neue Montreal
classification_notes: Versatile grotesque with the spirit of a display font; seven weights upright and italic for 14 styles, slightly tighter kerning, Cyrillic support. Inspired by Montreal's design city status, from Expo 67 to its contemporary scene.
summary: A grotesque with display spirit and tight kerning, in seven weights with italics and Cyrillic, pitched as a champion brand font.
description: PP Neue Montreal is a grotesque sans designed to act like a workhorse and a display face at once. Seven weights from Thin to Bold each have an italic, the kerning is slightly tighter than a default neutral grotesque, and the family carries Cyrillic support. The reference is Montreal as a design city, from Expo 67 to its current studio scene, and it has become one of the foundry's most-used identity faces.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Montreal as a versatile grotesque with the spirit of a display font, with 14 weights, slightly tighter kerning and Cyrillic support, pitched as your next champion font. They tie the design to what they see as Montreal's identity as a design city, from Expo 67 to its contemporary scene, and note its use by local brands including the rebrand of CF Montreal.

name: PP Neue Montreal Arabic
classification_notes: Arabic companion to PP Neue Montreal; seven weights plus a variable cut, with Cyrillic support carried over from the Latin family.
summary: An Arabic companion to Neue Montreal in seven weights with a variable cut, built for bilingual identity work.
description: PP Neue Montreal Arabic is the Arabic version of the foundry's most-used sans, drawn to sit beside the Latin family in bilingual identity work. It runs across seven weights plus a variable cut, carries Cyrillic support, and keeps the calm, slightly display-leaning tone of the parent grotesque. Use it where Arabic and Latin scripts need to feel like one voice rather than two systems.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram introduce this as the Arabic version of their most popular typeface, Neue Montreal, calling it a versatile grotesque with the spirit of a display font. They highlight 7 weights, Cyrillic support and the same Montreal-design-city heritage, and note its use by global and local brands.

name: PP Neue Montreal Mono
classification_notes: Monospaced version of PP Neue Montreal, with coding ligatures, multilingual support and a Button-mode feature; seven weights upright and italic for 14 styles plus a variable cut.
summary: A monospaced version of Neue Montreal with coding ligatures, multilingual support and a special button-mode for UI work.
description: PP Neue Montreal Mono is the monospace version of the foundry's bestseller, built for designers and developers who want their code voice to match their brand voice. It runs across seven weights upright and italic for 14 styles plus a variable cut, includes coding ligatures and a special Button-mode for UI work, and keeps the calm structural feel of the parent grotesque.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Montreal Mono as the perfect fusion of style and functionality and the monospace version of their bestseller, Neue Montreal. They flag coding ligatures, a Button-mode, 14 styles including italics and multilingual support as the reasons to upgrade your typography for design and code projects.

name: PP Neue World
classification_notes: Display serif inspired by vintage display types manually squeezed to fit print sizes; six widths and eight weights for 48 cuts plus a variable font. Free to try.
summary: A super-serif with six widths and eight weights, inspired by vintage display types squeezed to fit the page.
description: PP Neue World is a free-to-try super-serif designed as a width system for headlines and posters. Six widths, from Super Condensed to Extended, each carry eight weights for 48 cuts plus a variable font. The thinner weights are soft and refined, the heavy ones lean into hard contrast and bold statements, and the whole family is drawn to mimic vintage display serifs that were squeezed to fit a given print measure.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue World as a free-to-try super-serif font family with 6 widths and 48 cuts, inspired by vintage display types that were manually squeezed to fit the print size. They highlight soft curves and refined details in the thinner weights and a hard contrast in the heavier weights, and pitch it as a beautiful modern serif that pays homage to its forerunners.

name: PP Neue York
classification_notes: Sans serif drawn from New York vernacular signage, from bodega awnings to subway tile mosaics and worn American gothics; three widths (Normal, Narrow, Condensed) drawn with deliberately different x-heights, eight weights with italics, 48 styles total.
summary: A 48-style sans drawn from New York vernacular signage, in three widths with deliberately different x-heights for headline and text.
description: PP Neue York is a vernacular gothic sans built from the layered visual noise of New York: bodega awnings, worn American gothics, subway tile mosaics and avenue and street rhythm. It runs across three widths drawn with deliberately different x-heights, with the Condensed taller and tighter for headlines and the Normal proportioned for text and continuous reading. Eight weights with italics in each width give a 48-style family that flexes from display to running copy without breaking voice.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue York as a sans serif shaped by the city's layered visual noise: bodega awnings, worn American gothics, mosaic station tiles and the rhythm of avenues and streets. They frame it as a contemporary type system with a vintage pulse, clean in structure and gently rough around the edges, with three widths whose density shifts the way the city shifts block by block. They flag the family as 48 styles, eight weights with italics across three widths, with x-heights tuned for display and text use.

name: PP Nikkei
designer: Caio Kondo, Francesca Bolognini, Mat Desjardins
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Geometric, Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif, Text, Variable. Built as five interlinked typographic systems: Upright, Italic, Journal, Maru, Pacific, Line, with a Crests glyph set. Marketed as a tribute to Japanese immigration to America.
summary: A multi-system display family by Inari Type and Pangram, built as a typographic tribute to Japanese immigration to America.
