description: PP Nikkei is a sprawling display system by Caio Kondo, Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins, structured as five interlinked subfamilies plus a Crests glyph set. The complete collection spans 72 styles with italics, variable cuts and a dedicated set of Japanese family crests. Pangram positions it as a tribute to Nikkei communities, drawing on hand-lettered ship signage, immigrant newspapers and family crests for the design references.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Nikkei as a tribute to Japanese immigration to America through typography, honouring Nikkei communities and their descendants. Five typographic systems were drawn, each carrying a distinct concept but interlaced by a shared historical narrative. References cited include hand-lettered ship signage, Nikkei newspapers and Japanese family crests.

name: PP Object Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Geometric, Grotesk, Italics, Variable. Sixteen styles with italics and Cyrillic support. Pitched as a multifunctional workhorse blending Swiss neo-grotesk and geometric traits.
summary: A geometric neo-grotesque workhorse by Alex Slobzheninov, sized for branding, packaging, posters and small body text.
description: Object Sans is a 16-style sans by Alex Slobzheninov that blends Swiss neo-grotesk discipline with geometric construction. Seven weights run from Thin to Black, each paired with a Slanted cut, and the regular weight is tuned for small body text. Cyrillic support and a variable axis make it a useful default for identity systems, packaging and on-screen interfaces.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Object Sans as a contemporary type family that combines the best qualities of Swiss neo-grotesks and geometric fonts. They pitch it as a multifunctional workhorse for printed and on-screen contexts, including logo design, brand identities, websites, packaging, posters and headlines, with the regular weight tuned for small body text.

name: PP Pangaia
designer: Samuel Salminen, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Italics, Organic, Serif, Variable. Eighteen styles across nine weights with italics and a variable cut. Marketed as a free to try organic serif inspired by natural forms.
summary: An organic serif by Samuel Salminen with Pangram, drawn from natural forms and shipped in 18 styles plus a variable cut.
description: Pangaia is an organic serif of nine weights and matching italics, designed by Samuel Salminen with Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini. The forms blend traditional serif structure with curves and swashes drawn from leaves, riverbanks and rolling landscape. With 18 styles, swash italics and a variable cut, it stretches across book setting, branding and editorial display.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Pangaia as a free to try typeface that blends the splendour of traditional serifs with the rough, raw beauty of nature. They cite leaves, riverbanks and mountain slopes as inspirations, and frame the family as a celebration of our connection to the Earth across 18 styles, including striking swashes and powerful italics.

name: PP Pangram Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Geometric, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Six widths, 24 styles each, totalling 144 styles. Variable slanting from Reclined to Italic, with Cyrillic support.
summary: A 144-style geometric sans by Pangram with six widths and a variable slant axis from Reclined to Italic.
description: Pangram Sans is a geometric workhorse by Mat Desjardins and Valerio Monopoli, built across six widths and 24 styles each for 144 styles in total. A fully variable slant axis runs from Reclined to Italic, and Cyrillic support is included. The width range, from Compressed up to Normal, covers everything from masthead headlines to text setting in one system.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Pangram Sans as a powerful and extensive geometric workhorse, designed to be both bold and intense and subtle and flexible. They highlight the fully variable slant from Reclined to Italic, Cyrillic support, and the inspiration drawn from some of the greatest geometric typefaces.

name: PP Pangram Sans Rounded
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Geometric, Italics, Sans Serif. The rounded sibling of Pangram Sans, with the same 144-style six-width architecture and Cyrillic support.
summary: The rounded sibling of Pangram Sans, again with 144 styles across six widths, tuned for friendlier brand work.
description: Pangram Sans Rounded is the softened version of Pangram Sans by Mat Desjardins and Valerio Monopoli. It keeps the same six-width, 144-style architecture and Cyrillic support, with rounded terminals giving the heavier weights a more approachable feel. Useful where Pangram Sans would feel too austere, particularly in consumer brand work, packaging and apps.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Pangram Sans Rounded as the more fun and playful sibling of Pangram Sans, an extensive geometric family of 144 styles with a softer side. They note that the bolder weights are where the typeface becomes most distinctive, and the family includes Cyrillic support and free to try licensing.

name: PP Pier Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif. Ten styles across five weights with italics. Pitched as a slightly off geometric workhorse for everyday text and design needs.
summary: A ten-style geometric grotesque by Mat Desjardins, drawn to read well at both display and small text sizes.
description: Pier Sans is an early Pangram release by Mat Desjardins, structured as five weights with matching italics. The forms sit between geometric and grotesque, with detail tuned to hold up at both large and small sizes. It works as a quiet brand and interface sans where you want a touch of personality without leaving the workhorse register.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Pier as a modern, structured Grotesk with details that give it a unique personality. They built it to look as good at large display sizes as at very small ones, designed to fit everyday text and design needs.

name: PP Rader
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Brutalist, Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Sixteen styles across eight weights with italics and a variable cut. Drawn from vintage road signage and turn-of-the-century industrial grotesques.
summary: An expressive industrial grotesque by Valerio Monopoli, drawn from vintage road signage in 16 styles plus a variable cut.
description: Rader is a 16-style industrial grotesque by Valerio Monopoli, with weights from Hairline to Bold and matching italics. The drawing pulls from vintage German road signage and a debut-de-siecle aesthetic, sitting between editorial display and an expressive workhorse. Round inner counters give it a printed feel without losing its modern read.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Rader as both an expressive display typeface and a readable editorial tool, with 16 weights from a stripped-down hairline to a decorative bold. They cite vintage signage systems as the brief, framing the typeface as an attempt to bring craft back to wayfinding while spicing up the genre of industrial grotesques.

name: PP Radio Grotesk
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif. Sixteen styles across eight weights with italics and Cyrillic support. Pitched as a contemporary take on a classic Grotesk.
summary: A contemporary grotesque by Jack Harvatt and Mat Desjardins, 16 styles across eight weights with Cyrillic support.
description: Radio Grotesk is a 16-style contemporary grotesque by Jack Harvatt and Mat Desjardins. Eight weights run from Ultralight to Black with matching italics, and the family includes Cyrillic support. Established uppercase forms sit alongside more sophisticated lowercase, making it a steady choice for editorial and brand systems that want a current grotesque without trend baggage.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Radio Grotesk as a typeface that brings contemporary characteristics to a classic Grotesk style, pairing established majuscules with sophisticated minuscules. They highlight the eight weights as easily adaptable to any project.

name: PP Right Didone
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Display, Italics, Serif, Variable. Eight subfamilies with seven cuts each, 56 styles total. Built by adding serifs to Right Sans rather than as a historical revival, giving it a tall x-height and grotesk-like style range.
summary: A contemporary didone by Alex Slobzheninov, 56 styles built by adding serifs to Right Sans rather than reviving a historical model.
description: Right Didone is a 56-style high-contrast serif by Alex Slobzheninov, part of the Right superfamily. It is structured as eight cuts, including Tall, Tight, Compact, Casual, Wide, Narrow, Spatial and Italic, each with seven weights. Pangram frame the design as Right Sans with serifs added rather than a historical revival, giving it the tall x-height and broad style coverage of a grotesque rather than a classic didone.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Didone as the classic serif reinvented through a contemporary lens, beaming elegance with a slightly quirky manner. They make the point that it is more than not a historical revival, having been created by adding serifs to Right Sans, which is why it carries a tall x-height and a grotesk-like range of styles.

name: PP Right Gothic
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Gothic, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Seven subfamilies of 14 styles each, 98 styles total: 7 weights x 7 widths x 20 degree italic. Cyrillic support included. Drawn with pronounced contrast that nods to early pre-Swiss sans typefaces.
summary: A 98-style gothic sans by Alex Slobzheninov with seven weights, seven widths and pronounced thick-thin contrast.
description: Right Gothic is a 98-style sans by Alex Slobzheninov, built as seven weights across seven widths with italic cuts. Compared to the wider Right Grotesk, it carries a pronounced thick-thin contrast that nods to early pre-Swiss sans typefaces. Smooth ink traps and a contemporary feel keep it usable as a body voice as well as a headline. Cyrillic support is included.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Gothic as a large multi-purpose variable family with a unique voice rooted in distinct details, smooth ink traps and a contemporary feel. They explain that, unlike most modern sans serifs, Right Gothic features a clearly pronounced contrast between thick and thin strokes, referencing the pre-Swiss gothic genre of sans typefaces. The family covers 98 styles with optional custom contrast axes on request.

name: PP Right Grotesk
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Eleven subfamilies of 14 styles each, 130 styles total: 7 display cuts plus 5 text cuts. Cyrillic support included. The flagship neo-grotesque of the Right superfamily.
summary: A 130-style neo-grotesque by Alex Slobzheninov, with display and text cuts across seven widths and seven weights.
description: Right Grotesk is the anchor of Pangram's Right superfamily, a 130-style neo-grotesque by Alex Slobzheninov. Seven display cuts cover the wider Right system shapes, and five additional text cuts are tuned for paragraph reading. Moderate contrast, smooth curves and slightly unusual anatomy give it more personality than a strict workhorse, with Cyrillic support throughout.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Grotesk as a typeface that blends the neutrality and functionality of workhorses with a good touch of distinctive personality. They cite fine details, smooth curves, moderate contrast and slightly unusual anatomy, and frame it as not trendy and not timeless either, designed to be just Right.

name: PP Right Grotesk Mono
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Monospace, Sans Serif, Variable. Fourteen styles across seven weights with italics. Pitched as a softer, more playful monospace counterpart to Right Grotesk.
summary: A 14-style monospace by Alex Slobzheninov, pairing Right Grotesk's voice with playful technical details.
description: Right Grotesk Mono is a 14-style monospace by Alex Slobzheninov, built as seven weights from Fine to Black with matching italics. Its compact proportions read well in paragraphs while staying recognisable at large sizes, giving it a friendly technological feel. It pairs cleanly with Right Grotesk and Right Serif Mono, and slots into the broader Right superfamily.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Grotesk Mono as a typeface that spices up the rigid structure of the monospaced genre with playful details and subtle twists. They highlight its fairly compact proportions for paragraph reading and its place inside the wider Right superfamily, with Right Grotesk and Right Serif Mono cited as natural pairings.

name: PP Right Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Seven subfamilies of 14 styles each, 98 styles total. A high-contrast sans sibling to Right Grotesk and Right Gothic; together the three families exceed 300 fonts. Cyrillic support included.
summary: A 98-style high-contrast sans by Alex Slobzheninov, sitting between Right Grotesk and Right Gothic in the Right superfamily.
description: Right Sans is a 98-style high-contrast sans by Alex Slobzheninov, structured as seven widths and seven weights with italic cuts. Pangram describe it as fancier than your average sans without crossing into serif territory. Together with Right Grotesk and Right Gothic it forms a pairing system of more than 300 styles, with Cyrillic support across the family.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Sans as just a tiny bit fancier than your favourite serif fonts, except it keeps it sans, available across weights, widths and italic angles in classic and variable formats. They position it as the high-contrast sibling of Right Grotesk and Right Gothic, with the three families together giving over 300 fonts perfect for pairing.

name: PP Right Serif
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Display, Italics, Serif, Variable. Eight subfamilies of seven styles each, 56 styles total. Drawn by adding serifs to Right Gothic, inheriting moderate contrast, a tall x-height and grotesk-style style coverage rather than a classical revival lineage.
summary: A 56-style contemporary serif by Alex Slobzheninov, drawn by adding serifs to Right Gothic rather than reviving a historical model.
description: Right Serif is a 56-style serif by Alex Slobzheninov, built as seven weights across seven widths plus a dedicated Italic family. It originated as a sans, growing serifs onto Right Gothic, which is why it carries a moderate contrast, tall x-height and the broad style coverage more typical of a grotesque. Most useful at large and medium sizes, with regular cuts that hold up in smaller paragraphs.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Serif as a funky multitool, a spiced-up workhorse stretching between high function and unique personality. They make the point that, unlike many serifs rooted in history, Right Serif originated as a sans-serif growing serifs from Right Gothic, inheriting a tall x-height and a wide range of variable styles across seven weights and seven widths.

name: PP Right Serif Mono
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Monospace, Serif and Variable. It sits inside the wider Right superfamily, taking the proportions of Right Serif and refitting every glyph to a single fixed width.
summary: A serif monospace by Alex Slobzheninov that takes the Right superfamily into fixed-width territory without the usual deadpan mono feel.
description: A serif monospace built off the wider Right system, with seven weights from Fine to Black plus matching italics for fourteen styles in a variable file. Equal widths give it the grid discipline of code, but the proportions lean into the quirks of the Right family so paragraphs and large display lines keep some warmth. Useful for identity systems that want a bit of tech meets editorial, creative coding work, zines and infographics, and it pairs cleanly with Right Serif or Right Grotesk Mono.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Serif Mono as a monospace with the right twist, bringing the liveliness of the Right superfamily into the monospaced genre. They say it embraces the twisted proportions of every character fitted to one width while staying easy to read in paragraphs and distinctive at display size, and works equally well as a primary face or paired with Right Serif and Right Grotesk Mono.

name: PP Right Slab
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Display, Italics, Serif and Variable. It is the slab serif sibling of Right Grotesk, sharing low contrast and a large x-height, with seven weights across seven widths.
summary: A wide-ranging slab serif by Alex Slobzheninov, sibling to Right Grotesk, with 49 styles across seven weights and seven widths.
description: A low-contrast slab serif built as the serif brother of Right Grotesk, with chunky bracketed serifs and a large x-height. Seven weights run across seven widths from Tall to Spatial for 49 cuts in total, plus an italic family, all available in a variable file. Most at home at display size but the regular weights handle paragraphs, with a campaign by Studio Nari leaning into a cinematic, slightly Tarantino-flavoured mood.
foundry_blurb: Pangram call Right Slab the big boi of the family, massive at first glance and quickly familiar. They say its heavy styles unapologetically occupy the space with chunky serifs while light and narrow cuts leave more air, that it is the serif brother of Right Grotesk inheriting low contrast, large x-height and a ton of variable styles across seven weights and seven widths, and they paired the release with an American Psycho flavoured campaign from Studio Nari.

name: PP Stellar Sans
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Brutalist, Grotesk and Sans Serif. A slightly condensed sans with a tall x-height, designed for tight layouts and high-density settings.
summary: A slightly condensed grotesque sans by Mat Desjardins with a tall x-height, built for tight layouts and dense typographic systems.
description: A condensed sans serif drawn for legibility in tight measures, with a tall x-height that holds clarity at small sizes. Four weights from Light to Bold cover most hierarchy needs, and the narrow proportions make it useful for editorial spreads, mobile interfaces, packaging and poster blocks where every column counts. The brutalist edge keeps it from feeling generic when scaled up for display.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Stellar as a slightly condensed sans serif with a tall x-height for legibility and personality, designed for clarity, impact and efficient use of space. They frame it as inspired by the tension between the galactic and the atomic, with four weights built to give purpose to ideas at any scale.

name: PP Supply
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Geometric, Monospace, Sans Serif and Variable. A two-cut system with a Sans family and a matching Monospace family, sharing the same skeleton.
summary: A geometric sans plus matching monospace by Mat Desjardins, drawn from industrial design and architecture for utilitarian, structured layouts.
description: A two-part system pairing a geometric sans with a monospace cut on the same skeleton, eight weights each from Thin to Black with a variable file per cut. Precise curves and sharp angles give it an industrial feel that suits technical brands, packaging, dashboards and code-adjacent work where you want the structure of a mono to live alongside text in the sans. A reliable workhorse rather than a flashy display face.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Supply as a solid and versatile typeface inspired by industrial design and architecture, with precise curves and sharp angles. They pitch it as one for every designer's toolbox, available in two cuts, a Sans Serif and a classic Monospace, eight styles each plus variable fonts.

name: PP Talisman
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Display, Humanist, Italics, Sans Serif and Variable. A five-width, nine-weight humanist sans with matching obliques, organised across Compressed, Condensed, Narrow, Compact and Normal widths.
summary: A 90-style humanist display sans by Valerio Monopoli, spanning five widths and nine weights with matching obliques.
description: A humanist display sans built for breadth, with five widths from Compressed to Normal and nine weights from Thin to Black, plus matching obliques, totalling 90 styles each carrying 687 glyphs. The casual, stripped-back skeleton keeps it minimal at body size, while the wide range across widths lets brands run from compact captions to full-bleed posters within one system. Visuals for the release are by Sergio Lairisa.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Talisman as a lucky font to carry around, framing it as an attempt to harness luck through hyper-minimalist design with an effortless, almost simplistic structure. They say its generous range of weights and widths ensures variety of expression, made more vibrant by a dashing oblique counterpart, and pitch it as an amulet a designer keeps in their pocket as both first and last creative resort.

name: PP Telegraf
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Brutalist, Grotesk, Italics and Variable. A modern grotesk whose counters become more rectangular as weight increases, with eight weights and two italic systems at 15 degrees and 30 degrees.
summary: A brutalist grotesk by Nick Losacco and Samuel Salminen with rectangular counters in the heavy weights and two italic angles for slant or shock.
description: A modern grotesk that swaps grotesque softness for rigid angles, with counters becoming more rectangular as weight increases to keep small sizes legible and big sizes punchy. Twenty-four styles split across an Upright, a comfortable 15 degree Slanted for editorial, and a more aggressive 30 degree Oblique for graphic disruption, all with a variable file. Suits tech-forward brands, editorial systems and poster work that wants structure rather than warmth.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Telegraf as grotesk with grit, combining the forms of mid-century grotesks with rigid angles. They say its counters become more rectangular as weight increases to help on-screen viewing at small sizes and impact at large ones, framing it as a well-planted modern grotesk with a touch of brutalism, with a comfortable 15 degree Slanted for editorial work and an extreme 30 degree Oblique for a more brutalist, graphic approach.

name: PP Valve
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif and Variable. A two-family system pairing an ink-trapped Plain workhorse with a Stencil companion that exposes the modular structure, with three stylistic sets covering monospaced, humanist and cursive flavours.
summary: An industrial grotesque by Valerio Monopoli with an ink-trapped Plain family and a matching Stencil cut, plus monospaced, humanist and cursive stylistic sets.
description: A two-family sans serif system with a Plain cut built for small-size readability through ink traps and a Stencil cut that exposes the modular bones underneath. Sixteen styles per family with italics, plus three main stylistic sets covering a blocky semi-monospaced variant, a humanist style and a cursive option, each with contextual ligatures that adapt to tracking. Useful range from bold branding and signage to refined editorial.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Valve as a versatile collection that blends grotesque, geometric, gestural, industrial, monospaced and cursive sans serif styles into a unified ensemble. They highlight an ink-trapped workhorse for small-size readability and a striking stencil companion that reveals the modular structure, plus three stylistic sets with contextual ligatures that adapt to tracking, with visuals by Draaav, 3D by Reggy and motion by Andres Gutierrez Pineros.

name: PP Watch
designer: Niklas Herrmann, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Brutalist, Grotesk, Sans Serif and Variable. A wide-set sans inspired by Swiss watchmaking and engraved dial typography, with integrated small caps and a micro-serif stylistic set.
summary: A wide luxury sans by Niklas Herrmann, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, drawn from Swiss watch dial typography with built-in small caps.
description: A wide-set sans with calm horizontal posture and tight modulation, drawn from engraved watch face typography and skeleton dials in Swiss museum archives. Nine weights from Thin to Black with 994 glyphs each in a single variable file, including integrated small caps for traditional dial behaviour and an optional micro-serif stylistic set that nods to vintage ink traps. Aimed at luxury brands, high-end editorial and identity work that wants understated craft.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Watch as understated and overdelivered, a typeface born from the intricate world of horology and inspired by the fine detailing of Swiss timepieces and engraved watch face typography. They say it balances mechanical structure with luxurious refinement through a wide-set posture and tight modulation, with built-in small caps as a nod to traditional dials and a micro-serif stylistic set that echoes vintage ink-trap techniques, developed with Short Notice Studio.

name: PP Woodland
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Organic and Serif. A curvy serif with around 2,500 glyphs per style, soft modulation and quirky details such as a twisted lowercase e.
summary: A curvy organic serif by Mat Desjardins in six weights, with a quirky lowercase e, ligatures and curvier alternates for editorial and brand work.
description: A serif with smooth, organic curves and quiet quirks, sitting between elegance and warmth across six weights from Ultralight to Heavy. Each style carries roughly 2,500 glyphs, with ligatures, old-style figures and a stylistic set of curvier alternates so the same family can hold a calm body text or a more theatrical headline. Useful for luxury brands, literary magazines and contemporary editorial.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Woodland as a beautiful, curvy, versatile serif typeface with six weights and around 2,500 glyphs per style. They highlight the soft quirkiness in details such as the lowercase e, saying it gives the type a great personality in titles and body texts while keeping legibility, and note the elegant ligatures and curvier alternates available when more personality is needed.

name: PP Writer
subcategory: Renaissance serif with Text and Display cuts
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Cyrillic, Display, Italics, Serif, Text and Variable. A French Renaissance inspired serif with three cuts, Text, Display and Italic, and Cyrillic support that follows Cyrillic rather than Latin tradition on letters such as Tse and Sha.
summary: A Renaissance-inspired serif by Fred Wiltshire and Mat Desjardins with Text, Display and Italic cuts, nine weights, Cyrillic support and 30 styles in total.
description: A French Renaissance serif with proportions, contrast and stress drawn from sixteenth-century type, organised into a Text cut for 6 to 14 point body, a Display cut that sharpens contrast and serifs for big sizes, and a lively Italic. Thirty styles in total with around 744 glyphs each, plus a Cyrillic that respects local tradition on letters like Tse and Sha rather than copying Latin shapes. A workhorse for print and editorial that still has a bit of ink-spill drama in the heavier weights.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Writer as a French Renaissance inspired serif, an elegant and dynamic face including three cuts, text and display styles plus a playful italic, with nine weights per cut and 744 glyphs each. They frame it as a legible, traditional text face that pushes Renaissance type into the 21st century, with a Cyrillic that follows Cyrillic tradition rather than Latin motifs on letters such as Tse and Sha.

name: Palma
designer: Alex Slobzheninov, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Palma blends geometric and humanist sans-serif qualities across its two subfamilies, with open shapes and natural proportions in Plain and tighter, more geometric forms in Fizzy. The foundry tags it as both humanist and display, reflecting its dual nature.
summary: A 28-style dual-nature sans spanning a restrained humanist cut and a crisper, more geometric Fizzy variant, drawing loose inspiration from Antique Olive, Johnston and Avenir.
description: Designed by Alex Slobzheninov, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, Palma splits into two subfamilies that share the same weight range but diverge in character. The Plain family favours open, legible forms suited to running text and subtle applications, while Fizzy tightens spacing and accentuates detail for use at display sizes. Both subfamilies ship in seven weights with matching italics and variable font files, covering a broad range of Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe PP Palma as a spritzy sans of a dual geo-humanist nature, loosely inspired by Antique Olive, Johnston and Avenir but taking a lighthearted route with its own flair. The Plain subfamily offers reading comfort and legibility for subtle roles, while Fizzy cranks up geometric flavour for large-size, expressive use. The full collection spans 28 styles across two subfamilies of seven weights plus italics, all available in variable format with OpenType features and wide Latin language support.

name: Paloma
designer: Mat Desjardins, Alex Slobzheninov, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Display, Expressive, Humanist, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The family carries two subfamilies, Plain and Fizzy, that share a skeleton but split between humanist body settings and tighter geometric display cuts.
summary: A 28-style dual geo-humanist sans by Mat Desjardins, with a humanist Plain cut and a tighter, more geometric Fizzy cut.
description: A dual geo-humanist sans system by Mat Desjardins, with Alex Slobzheninov and Francesca Bolognini credited on the project. The 28-style family splits into two subfamilies: Plain, with open shapes, natural letterforms and balanced proportions for reading-comfort settings, and Fizzy, which tightens spacing, geometricises the shapes and crisps the details for large-size display. Each subfamily runs seven weights with italics, plus variable cuts. Pangram describe it as loosely inspired by Antique Olive, Johnston and Avenir, but treating those references lightheartedly. Useful where a brand needs both a comfortable text voice and a punchier display sibling without leaving the family.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe PP Paloma as a spritzy sans family of a dual geo-humanist nature. They credit it as designed for versatility, spanning humanist and geometric genres in two subfamilies, and loosely inspired by Antique Olive, Johnston and Avenir but treated with a lighthearted flair. They count 28 styles across two subfamilies of seven weights plus italics, all available as variable fonts, with OpenType features for professional typesetting and wide Latin language support.

name: Pangaia
designer: Samuel Salminen, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangaia is classified as a serif with organic, nature-inspired curves; its fluid strokes and blossoming serifs draw from natural forms rather than conventional geometric or humanist traditions, placing it firmly in an expressive, organic serif subcategory.
summary: A nature-themed serif by Samuel Salminen, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, drawing its fluid curves and expressive swashes from organic forms found in the natural world.
description: Spanning nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics and a variable cut, Pangaia's letterforms reference leaves, riverbanks and mountain slopes through undulating strokes and serifs that widen like branches. Alternate glyphs for J, V and W alongside swash variants add further expressiveness suited to branding and editorial use. The 513-glyph set covers a broad range of Latin Extended languages including Croatian, Hungarian, Icelandic and Romanian.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Pangaia as a typeface blending traditional serif construction with the raw, organic beauty of nature. Drawing from the Earth's flora and fauna, its fluid curves evoke leaves, riverbanks and mountain slopes, while its swashes and italic cuts across 18 styles make it suited to brand, book and publishing applications.

name: Pangram Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans, with circular forms and consistent stroke widths characteristic of the geometric grotesque tradition. The variable slant axis and wide range of widths place it firmly in the geometric sans-serif category.
summary: A large geometric sans collection spanning six width variants and a variable slant axis, with full Cyrillic support across 144 styles.
description: Pangram Sans is a geometric sans built around consistency and range, offering six sub-families from Compressed to Normal, each with 24 styles and a variable cut. The variable font includes a continuous slant axis running from reclined to italic, giving designers fine control over angle without switching between separate files. Each sub-family includes Cyrillic support, making the collection serviceable across Latin and Cyrillic-script markets.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Pangram Sans as a powerful geometric workhorse designed to be as bold and intense as it is subtle and flexible. They highlight the fully variable slanting from reclined to italic, Cyrillic alphabet support, and the family's inspiration from classic geometric typefaces, positioning it as a go-to font for a wide range of design work.

name: Pangram Sans Rounded
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with rounded terminals, explicitly positioned as a friendlier variant of Pangram Sans. The rounded stroke endings and geometric construction place it firmly in the rounded geometric sans tradition.
summary: A rounded variant of Pangram Sans offering 144 styles across six width subfamilies, with variable font support and Cyrillic coverage.
description: Built on the same geometric skeleton as Pangram Sans, this family softens the original with rounded terminals to produce a warmer, more approachable character. Six subfamilies spanning Compressed through Normal each carry 24 styles plus a variable cut, with italic and Cyrillic support throughout. The bolder weights are where the rounded forms make their strongest impression, making the family well suited to branding and packaging work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Pangram Sans Rounded as the more fun and playful sibling of Pangram Sans, retaining a powerful and extensive geometric workhorse quality across 144 styles while adding a more approachable side. They note the typeface is particularly distinctive in its bolder weights and highlight its Cyrillic support, framing it as a go-to rounded font for designers looking to uplift their work.

name: Pier Sans
classification_notes: Pier Sans is described as a modern Grotesk rooted in geometric principles with deliberate asymmetric detailing, placing it firmly in the geometric grotesque tradition with humanising quirks.
summary: A geometric grotesque by Mat Desjardins with deliberate off-beat curves and micro-details intended to add warmth to an otherwise clean, structured form.
description: Pier Sans sits within the geometric grotesque tradition but pushes against pure sterility through subtle asymmetries and idiosyncratic curve decisions. Ten styles span five weights from Light to Black with matching italics, covering a broad Latin glyph set with support for over 30 languages. It targets brand identity, editorial and UI work where a grotesk needs some personality without sacrificing legibility at small sizes.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Pier Sans as a modern Grotesk designed for clarity, structure and subtle individuality, rooted in geometric principles but intentionally offset just enough to stand out. They position it as a versatile everyday typeface equally suited to large headlines and compact text, with thoughtful asymmetry and micro-details that reward close inspection.

name: Playground
classification_notes: A contemporary semi-connected script rooted in classical copperplate calligraphic tradition, drawn with pointed-nib rhythms and precise geometric construction. Pangram categorise it under Display, Expressive, Humanist, Italics and Serif.
summary: A contemporary copperplate script by Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins, mixing pointed-nib calligraphic tradition with geometric letter construction across five weights.
description: Rooted in the pointed-nib copperplate tradition, Playground introduces unexpected tensions through exaggerated stroke contrast, square forms alongside flourishing curves, and acute cuts paired with round shoulders. The font is semi-connected by default, with full cursive connection available via contextual alternates, and two sets of capitals covering both italic and swash variations. Five weights span Thin to Medium, with lighter cuts suited to display sizes and heavier cuts holding up at smaller settings.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Playground as a reinterpretation of classical copperplate calligraphic style, emerging from the intersection of pointed-nib pen rhythm, historical calligraphic reference and precise geometric letter construction. They position it within the 'Pangram Playground' world, where the seriousness of work is interrupted by experiment and play, with the elegance of the copperplate script deliberately disrupted by unexpected shapes and exaggerated contrast.

name: Radio Grotesk
classification_notes: Explicitly categorised as a Grotesk by the foundry, rooted in classic grotesque sans-serif forms with contemporary refinements to details and rhythm.
summary: A grotesque sans with 16 styles and a variable axis, drawing on classical grotesk structure with sharpened contemporary details and broad Cyrillic support.
description: Designed by Jack Harvatt and Mat Desjardins, Radio Grotesk pairs well-proportioned majuscules with refined minuscules across eight weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic. A variable font cut is included, and OpenType features cover alternate letterforms, round dots, discretionary ligatures and tabular figures. Language support spans Latin Extended and Cyrillic, covering over 30 languages including Russian and Serbian.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Radio Grotesk as a broadcast from the past refined for the present, rooted in trusted classic grotesque forms but updated with sharper details and smoother flow. They position it as equally at home in brand systems, editorial headlines and web interfaces, noting its balance between elegance at lighter weights and presence at heavier ones.

name: Right Didone
classification_notes: A high-contrast serif in the Didone tradition, characterised by dramatic thick-thin stroke contrast and fine serifs, though its origins in Right Sans and its large x-height give it an atypical grotesk-influenced structure.
summary: A high-contrast Didone serif built by adding serifs to Right Sans, spanning eight width-based sub-families with variable fonts throughout.
description: Created by adding serifs to the grotesk Right Sans and smoothing the result, Right Didone inherits a large x-height and optical consistency more typical of a sans than a classical Didone revival. Eight sub-families cover a broad range of widths from Narrow and Compact to Wide and Spatial, each offering seven styles plus a variable cut, totalling 56 styles across the collection. Its intended territory is display work: posters, magazine headlines, logotypes and websites where strong contrast and scale make the thick-thin drama count.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Right Didone as a reinvention of the classic Didone serif through a contemporary lens, characterised by elegance with a slightly quirky edge. They note it was created against the natural flow of type design by adding serifs to Right Sans and smoothing the result, with a large x-height and broad range of styles borrowed from grotesk conventions. The campaign imagery, produced by Studio Nari, balances classic typographic motifs with bold colour and motion.

name: Right Gothic
classification_notes: Described as a 'gothic' sans referring to the pre-Swiss genre of early sans serifs, featuring pronounced stroke contrast reminiscent of serif anatomy, placing it clearly in the historical gothic/grotesque lineage rather than modern neutral sans territory.
summary: A large variable gothic sans by Alex Slobzheninov spanning seven widths and seven weights, distinguished by a pronounced stroke contrast drawn from early pre-Swiss sans tradition.
description: Right Gothic spans 98 static styles across seven subfamilies — Tall, Tight, Narrow, Compact, Casual, Wide and Spatial — each available in 14 styles with a variable cut covering weight, width and italic axes. Unlike most contemporary sans serifs, it reintroduces visible thick-to-thin contrast, referencing the anatomy of early grotesques that sat between serif and the later neutralised Swiss model. Cyrillic support is included across all subfamilies.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Right Gothic as a large multi-purpose variable type family with a distinct voice built on smooth ink traps, a contemporary feel and familiar letterforms. They position its pronounced stroke contrast as a deliberate step back towards serif anatomy, and note that 'Gothic' in the name refers to the genre of early pre-Swiss sans typefaces — serifs without serifs. The full collection covers 98 styles across seven subfamilies, each with its own variable font, with a custom contrast axis available on request.

name: Right Grotesk
classification_notes: A grotesque sans-serif with moderate contrast and slightly unusual anatomical details, sitting between neutral workhorse grotesques and more characterful designs. Pangram classify it under Grotesk and Sans Serif.
summary: A large grotesque collection by Alex Slobzheninov spanning 11 sub-families and 130 styles, built to work across display and text settings with a mild personality that sits between neutral and characterful.
description: Right Grotesk is structured around seven display cuts and four text cuts, each available in 14 or 8 styles respectively, plus variable font versions, with full Cyrillic support throughout. Smooth curves, moderate contrast and small anatomical quirks give it enough character to distinguish itself from pure utility grotesques without tipping into eccentricity. The width range, from Narrow and Compact through to Wide and Spatial, makes it adaptable across branding, editorial and screen work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Right Grotesk as blending the neutrality and functionality of workhorses with a good touch of distinctive personality, featuring fine details, smooth curves, moderate contrast and slightly unusual anatomy. They position it as capable of being a loud hero or a humble supporting actor, designed to be versatile and high-quality for both serious and fun projects, and intentionally neither trendy nor timeless.

name: Right Grotesk Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width grotesque sans-serif with humanist warmth; Pangram Pangram classify it under Grotesk, Monospace, and Sans Serif, placing it firmly in the monospaced grotesque tradition.
summary: A fixed-width grotesque by Alex Slobzheninov that trades monospaced rigidity for compact proportions and subtle personality, available in seven weights with matching italics and a variable cut.
description: Part of Pangram Pangram's broader Right superfamily, it brings a friendlier register to the typically austere monospaced genre through compact proportions and a handful of alternate characters including double-storey a and g options, circled numerals and case-sensitive forms. Fourteen styles span Fine to Black across upright and italic, with a variable font also included. It pairs directly with Right Grotesk and Right Serif Mono within the superfamily for mixed-weight editorial and UI systems.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Right Grotesk Mono as taking the strict fixed-width skeleton of a monospaced typeface and breathing life into it, with compact proportions, subtle quirks and a friendly tech-forward vibe suited to UI layouts, poster design, edgy branding and editorial systems. They note it pairs naturally with Right Grotesk and Right Serif Mono, and that it is available as a variable font with 508 glyphs per style across 14 styles released in December 2023.

name: Right Sans
classification_notes: A sans-serif with notably high stroke contrast, described by the foundry as the 'graceful cousin' in the Right family alongside Right Grotesk and Right Gothic, occupying a refined yet unseriffed space.
summary: A high-contrast sans-serif by Alex Slobzheninov offered across seven width-based subfamilies, each with 14 styles and a variable cut, covering Latin and Cyrillic.
description: Designed by Alex Slobzheninov and released in 2022, Right Sans brings a degree of stroke contrast rarely found in sans-serifs, positioning it between its siblings Right Grotesk and Right Gothic as the more refined option in the Right family. Seven subfamilies span Tall through to Spatial, offering 98 styles in total alongside variable font files. Language support covers the majority of Latin and Cyrillic-based scripts, making it a workable choice for multilingual editorial and branding projects.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Right Sans as the graceful cousin in the Right family, offering high contrast and versatility with just the right amount of flair. They position it as a slightly fancier take on a sans-serif, available across a huge range of weights, widths and italic angles in both classic and variable formats, supporting the majority of Latin and Cyrillic-based languages.

name: Right Serif
classification_notes: Originated as a sans-serif (Right Gothic) with serifs grafted on, inheriting large x-height, moderate contrast and grotesk proportions across 7 weights and 7 widths — placing it firmly in the grotesque-influenced serif territory rather than traditional historical models.
summary: A grotesk-derived serif with large x-height and unusual anatomy, spanning 8 sub-families across 7 weights and 7 widths with variable font support.
description: Grown directly from Right Gothic by adding serifs, this family inherits grotesk traits — large x-height, moderate contrast and broad width range — that distinguish it from historically rooted serif designs. Eight sub-families cover Tall, Tight, Narrow, Compact, Casual, Wide, Spatial and Italic cuts, each offering 7 styles plus a variable font, totalling 56 static styles. Styles near Regular are usable at text sizes, though the family is primarily suited to display and campaign work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Right Serif as a sans-serif that grew serifs, inheriting moderate contrast and large x-height from its grotesk origins in Right Gothic. They position it as a versatile workhorse with a distinct personality, spanning 7 weights and 7 widths across 8 sub-families, and associate it visually with a fashion campaign aesthetic inspired by minimalist luxury labels.

name: Right Serif Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with serif detailing, drawn as part of the Right superfamily. Equal-width spacing is the defining structural constraint, with serif letterforms giving it a character distinct from utilitarian code fonts.
summary: A serif monospaced typeface by Alex Slobzheninov that extends the Right superfamily into fixed-width territory across 14 styles and a variable axis.
description: Part of the Right superfamily, this typeface applies serif character and geometric discipline to the equal-width constraints of a monospaced format. Seven weights from Fine to Black each have a matching italic, and the full family is also available as a variable font. The design works at text sizes without losing legibility, and holds enough personality at display sizes for identity and editorial use alongside Right Serif or Right Grotesk Mono.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Right Serif Mono as what happens when a monospaced typeface decides it doesn't have to be boring. It brings the charm of the Right superfamily into the monospaced genre, embracing the quirky proportions that come from fitting every letter into the same width while keeping things readable in paragraphs and distinctive at display sizes. They position it for identity systems needing a tech-meets-editorial feel, creative coding visuals, zines and infographics, and note it pairs naturally with Right Serif and Right Grotesk Mono.

name: Right Slab
classification_notes: A low-contrast slab serif with chunky serifs, large x-height and extended forms designed primarily for display use, classifying it clearly as a display slab. The wide variable axis coverage and multiple width subfamilies reinforce its display orientation.
summary: A chunky, low-contrast display slab spanning eight subfamilies and seven widths, designed by Alex Slobzheninov as a serif companion to Right Grotesk.
description: Built on the same structural DNA as Right Grotesk, Right Slab shares its low contrast, large x-height and extensive variable coverage across 7 weights and 7 widths. Eight subfamilies from Tall to Spatial give designers precise control over proportion, with an Italic family rounding out the collection at 56 static styles plus variable fonts. The visual campaign positions it against vintage cinema aesthetics, which suits its heavy styles well, though lighter cuts can handle short text passages.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Right Slab as a slab serif brother to Right Grotesk, inheriting its low contrast and large x-height while adding chunky serifs and extended forms with serious screen presence. Framed around a fictional cinematic campaign they dubbed 'American Psycho' Slab, the family is designed by Alex Slobzheninov and covers 8 subfamilies with 7 styles each, plus variable cuts, making it suitable for display work from bold headlines down to near-regular text sizes.

name: Räder
classification_notes: Räder is an industrial grotesque with roots in vintage road signage, featuring round inner counters and a modern skeletal form. Pangram Pangram categorise it as brutalist and expressive, but its structure is fundamentally grotesque.
summary: A grotesque sans by Valerio Monopoli drawing from vintage road signage, with round inner counters that give it a printed, tactile quality beneath a modern industrial frame.
description: Designed by Valerio Monopoli and released in January 2022, Räder spans 16 styles across eight weights from Hairline to Bold, each with a true italic. Its round inner counters recall the craft of pre-neutral wayfinding systems, where legibility and visual character were not treated as opposing concerns. A variable font is included, and an extensive set of OpenType alternates covers letterforms such as A, K, R, W and Q, giving designers room to shift the register from restrained editorial to something more characterful.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Räder as a typeface that bridges expressive display use with editorial clarity, spanning 16 carefully crafted weights from razor-sharp Hairline to decorative Bold. They draw its inspiration from vintage road signage, a time when wayfinding was both public service and craft, where designers balanced clarity of message with beauty of appearance. The inner round counters give it the feel of a printed, tactile face, while the overall form remains sharp and contemporary, making it, in their words, the best of both ages.

name: Stellar Sans
classification_notes: Pangram categorises it as Brutalist and Grotesk; it is a condensed sans-serif with a tall x-height and narrow proportions, sitting within the grotesque tradition with a utilitarian, space-efficient character.
summary: A condensed grotesque in four weights with a tall x-height, built for tight layouts and typographic hierarchies in editorial and brand work.
description: Narrow proportions and a raised x-height make this a practical choice where space is constrained, whether in mobile interfaces, packaging or dense editorial spreads. Four weights from Light to Bold allow basic hierarchy without the range needed for more complex typographic systems. The foundry cites a galactic-to-atomic design philosophy, which reads more as marketing flavour than a structurally distinctive feature of the letterforms.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Stellar Sans as a condensed sans-serif designed for clarity, impact and efficient use of space. They highlight its taller x-height for legibility in tight layouts and position it for editorial, web, branding and poster work. Its narrow proportions are intended to allow more content in less space, with four weights to support typographic hierarchy.

name: Supply
classification_notes: Described as inspired by industrial design and architecture with precise curves and sharp angles; the sans subfamily is a clean geometric sans, while the mono variant is monospaced. Classified as geometric sans for the primary cut.
summary: A geometric sans family by Mat Desjardins, drawn from industrial and architectural references, available in a sans and monospaced cut across eight weights each with variable font support.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins with sharp angles and precise curves informed by industrial design and architecture, Supply comes in two subfamilies: a geometric sans and a classic monospace. Each cut runs from Thin to Black across eight styles, with a variable font available for both, giving 360 glyphs per style. The pairing of sans and mono within a single family makes it well-suited to technical branding, editorial work and interface design.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Supply as a solid and versatile typeface inspired by industrial design and architecture, with precise curves and sharp angles making it a go-to for designers. It ships in two useful cuts, a sans serif and a monospace, each in eight styles plus a variable font, covering a wide range of structured design applications.

name: Talisman
classification_notes: Tagged by the foundry as both Display and Humanist Sans; its stripped-down, casual structure with oblique variants and broad width range places it firmly in the humanist sans-serif tradition, styled for display use.
summary: A hyper-minimalist humanist sans by Valerio Monopoli spanning five widths from Compressed to Normal, each in 18 styles with obliques and variable cuts.
description: Designed by Valerio Monopoli and updated in January 2024, Talisman stretches across five width subfamilies — Compressed, Condensed, Narrow, Compact and Normal — yielding 90 styles in total, each with 687 glyphs. Its structure is deliberately stripped back and casual, and the oblique cuts add rhythm across the range. Variable fonts are included with each subfamily, covering weight and width axes.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Talisman as an attempt to channel luck through hyper-minimalist design, with a casual and simplistic structure meant to embody effortlessness. They position it as a versatile workhorse for designers, equally useful as a first or last creative resort, made more expressive by its oblique counterpart and generous span of weights and widths.

name: Telegraf
classification_notes: Telegraf is firmly a grotesque sans-serif, drawing from mid-century grotesk traditions while incorporating sharp, rigid angles and rectangular counters that give it a distinctly brutalist character.
summary: A brutalist-leaning grotesque by Nick Losacco and Samuel Salminen, offering eight weights with two distinct angled cuts at 15° and 30°.
description: Telegraf takes mid-century grotesk forms and pulls them in a harder, more structural direction, with counters that become progressively more rectangular as weight increases. The family spans 24 styles across eight weights, with a 15° Slanted cut for editorial and long-form use and a more aggressive 30° Oblique for graphic, high-impact work. A variable font is included, and the glyph set covers a broad range of Latin Extended languages.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Telegraf as a modernist machine dressed in mid-century tailoring and built with brutalist bones, drawing from classic grotesks while replacing softness with sharp angles and rigid energy. They position it as suited to both tech-forward branding and indie editorial work, noting that the two italic cuts — a comfortable 15° Slanted and a full 30° Oblique — offer distinct registers for hierarchy and graphic disruption respectively.

name: Valve
classification_notes: Pangram Pangram categorise Valve as a grotesk sans serif; the collection blends grotesque, geometric, gestural, industrial, monospaced and cursive sans influences across two families, making it a broadly hybrid grotesque sans.
summary: A two-family grotesque collection by Valerio Monopoli, pairing an ink-trapped plain workhorse with a modular stencil companion, each available as a variable font across 16 styles.
description: Valve brings together grotesque, geometric, gestural and cursive sans influences under one collection, unified by a shared modular structure visible most clearly in the stencil family. Three stylistic sets run through each family: a blocky semi-monospaced variant, a humanist option and a lively cursive, all with contextual ligatures that adapt to tracking. The variable fonts expose weight and slant axes, making the collection practical across branding, signage and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Valve as a versatile collection that blends grotesque, geometric, gestural, industrial, monospaced and cursive sans styles into a cohesive ensemble. It comprises a plain ink-trapped family built for readability at small sizes and a stencil companion that exposes the font's modular structure, with three stylistic sets and contextual ligatures throughout. The collection is suited to bold branding, signage and editorial design.

name: Watch
designer: Niklas Herrmann, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: A wide-set grotesque sans with tight modulation and calm horizontal posture; Pangram categorise it as Brutalist/Grotesk/Sans Serif, with an optional micro-serif stylistic set that does not override its primary classification.
summary: A wide grotesque sans in nine weights, drawn with reference to Swiss watchmaking typography and the engraved lettering of classic dial faces.
description: Designed by Niklas Herrmann, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, Watch takes its formal cues from skeleton dials, vintage Swiss museum archives and the engraved lettering found on traditional watch faces. Its wide-set proportions and tight modulation produce a calm, controlled rhythm suited to headlines, logotypes and editorial settings. Integrated small caps, alternate glyphs and an optional micro-serif stylistic set extend its range, while a single variable font file spans all nine weights for technical flexibility.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Watch as a luxurious wide sans serif born from the world of Swiss watchmaking, shaped by obsessive attention to detail and drawing on engraved dial typography, skeleton dials and vintage museum archives. With 994 glyphs per style across nine weights and a variable file, they position it for luxury branding, high-end publications and identity work that values craft and understated sophistication.

name: Woodland
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry under Organic and Serif; the soft curves, quirky alternates and warm personality place it firmly in the organic serif subcategory rather than a classical or transitional tradition.
summary: A six-weight organic serif by Mat Desjardins with soft curves and a distinctive lowercase 'e', suited to editorial and brand work.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins, Woodland runs from Ultralight to Heavy across six weights, each carrying over 2,500 glyphs including old-style figures, standard ligatures and stylistic alternates with curvier forms. The quirky lowercase 'e' gives the family a gentle personality without disrupting readability at body sizes, and the alternates allow for a more expressive register when needed. Coverage extends to a broad range of Latin-based languages, making it practical for European multilingual projects.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Woodland as a beautiful, curvy and versatile serif with six weights and 2,500 glyphs crafted with close attention to detail. They highlight the soft quirkiness of the lowercase 'e' as giving the typeface great personality in both titles and body text, and point to the elegant ligatures and curvier alternates as options for when more personality is called for.

name: Writer
classification_notes: Writer draws directly from 16th-century French Renaissance type in its proportions, contrast, modulation and stress, placing it firmly in the humanist old-style tradition with distinctly Renaissance characteristics.
summary: A French Renaissance-inspired serif by Fred Wiltshire and Mat Desjardins, offered in three cuts across ten weights with Cyrillic support.
description: Built on the proportions, contrast and diagonal stress of 16th-century French Renaissance type, Writer comes in Display, Text and Italic cuts, each running from Thin to Black. The Text cut is optimised for sizes between 6 and 14pt, the Display cut sharpens and exaggerates the strokes for larger settings, and the Italic is drawn as a lively independent companion rather than a sloped roman. Each style contains 744 glyphs including small caps, discretionary ligatures, tabular figures and alternate Q forms, with Cyrillic following its own tradition rather than mimicking the Latin.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Writer as an elegant and dynamic serif drawing on French Renaissance type, designed to serve as a workhorse for print and editorial use. They note that while it is rooted in the past it pushes the Renaissance style into the 21st century, with the Display cut accentuating and exaggerating strokes, the Text cut built for small sizes, and the Italic described as lively and characterful. The Cyrillic extension is noted for respecting Cyrillic tradition rather than simply applying a Latin aesthetic.
